task_prompt: A fintech startup built a gradient boosting model for credit card fraud detection using 18 months of transaction data from a single issuing bank, totaling approximately 2.1 million transactions with a fraud rate of 0.3%. The team trained on the first 12 months and evaluated on the remaining 6 months. They compare their model against a rule-based system currently in production, which was hand-tuned by the bank's fraud analysts over several years. The rule-based system flags 1.2% of all transactions for review, capturing 61% of fraud cases. The team's model flags 0.9% of transactions and captures 74% of fraud cases, and they claim this represents a substantial operational improvement. To demonstrate that their model generalizes, the team evaluated on 10 independent random subsamples of the test period and found stable AUC values. The paper notes that the rule-based baseline was evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions. One detail buried in the appendix: the rule-based system was not retrained or re-tuned using the training period data used by the ML model; it is the live production system evaluated on historical data. The team argues that this is fair because the rules were designed using older data, just as their model was, and that the comparison reflects real-world deployment conditions. The team uses this comparison to advocate for replacing the rule-based system entirely in production.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline rule-based system was not given access to the same training window for re-tuning, creating an asymmetric comparison that inflates the ML model's apparent advantage.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-evaluate after allowing fraud analysts to re-tune rules using the same 12-month training window, then compare on the held-out test period.

planted_issues.description: The rule-based baseline was not retrained or re-tuned on the same 12-month training window available to the ML model, creating an unfair advantage.
Comparing a live production rule system (frozen in time) against a freshly trained ML model does not isolate the benefit of ML over rules; it partially measures recency of training.

ideal_critique: The rule-based system was not given the opportunity to incorporate the 12 months of training data, so any performance gap may reflect data recency rather than model architecture.
A valid comparison would retrain or re-tune the rule-based system on the same training window.
The comparison as structured may be measuring 'stale rules vs. fresh model' rather than 'rules vs. ML.'
ideal_defense: In real deployments, rule systems are rarely re-tuned with every new data window, so the comparison may reflect realistic conditions.
The temporal split (12 months train, 6 months test) is methodologically correct for fraud detection.
AUC stability across subsamples suggests the ML model's performance estimate is reliable.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After re-tuning rules on the same training window, the performance gap shrinks to under 5 percentage points in recall.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Re-tuned rules perform no better than the original rules, indicating the gap is due to ML's representational power, not data recency.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Re-tuning improves the rules somewhat but ML still leads, leaving open the question of how much of the gap is recency versus architecture.

must_not_claim: model is overfitting to the training period
AUC is wrong metric for fraud detection
the fraud rate is too low to train on

ground_truth_rationale: The core flaw is asymmetric training access: the ML model sees 12 months of fresh data while the rule system does not. This is a classic evaluation design flaw in applied fraud ML. The 10-subsample stability check is genuine and valid methodology, but does not address the baseline asymmetry.
notes: Easy case. Flaw is stated in the appendix detail; tests whether evaluators read carefully. Subsample AUC check is a valid red herring — it addresses a different concern.

task_prompt: A natural language processing team developed a transformer-based text classifier for detecting toxic comments on a social media platform. The dataset contains 50,000 comments, of which 2,500 (5%) are labeled toxic. The team fine-tuned a pre-trained BERT-base model and compared it against a majority-class baseline that always predicts 'non-toxic.' They report that their model achieves 97.2% accuracy versus the majority-class baseline's 95.0% accuracy, concluding that their model represents a 2.2 percentage point improvement. The team notes that they used stratified train/test splits and tuned hyperparameters on a held-out validation set. The paper highlights that the BERT model required only 4 hours of GPU training, making it highly practical. They argue that the 2.2% improvement in accuracy demonstrates the utility of pre-trained language models for toxicity detection. The classifier is intended to be deployed in a content moderation pipeline where flagging missed toxic comments carries a significantly higher cost than false positives. To demonstrate robustness, the team also reports that performance was consistent across three random seeds, with accuracy standard deviation below 0.3%. The team plans to use this as their production system and presents the accuracy results to stakeholders as the primary evidence of success. They acknowledge in a footnote that their dataset is imbalanced but state that accuracy is the standard metric in the toxicity detection literature and defer to it accordingly.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Accuracy is an inappropriate primary metric for a highly imbalanced toxicity detection task, and the majority-class baseline is trivially weak, masking the model's likely poor recall on toxic content.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report precision, recall, F1, and AUROC on the toxic class; compare against a TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline and a simple keyword filter baseline.

planted_issues.description: Accuracy is a misleading metric on a 95/5 imbalanced dataset; a majority-class predictor trivially achieves 95% without identifying any toxic content.
Comparing against a majority-class baseline is not a meaningful evaluation; the team should benchmark against a TF-IDF classifier or a prior deployed system.
Given that the team explicitly states false negatives (missed toxic comments) are more costly, no asymmetric loss metric or cost-sensitive evaluation is reported.

ideal_critique: The majority-class baseline is not a meaningful comparator; it detects zero toxic comments and yet scores 95% accuracy.
Accuracy on a 5% positive class dataset is nearly uninformative about the quality of positive-class detection.
The team's own admission that false negatives are more costly contradicts their choice of symmetric accuracy as the primary metric.
A proper baseline should be a logistic regression or keyword-based filter that actually attempts to classify toxic content.
ideal_defense: The team correctly used stratified splits and multiple random seeds, indicating some methodological care.
Consistency across seeds suggests the reported accuracy is not a statistical fluke.
BERT fine-tuning is a reasonable choice for this task; the architecture is not the problem.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Recall on the toxic class is below 50% or the TF-IDF baseline matches or exceeds BERT on F1.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: AUROC exceeds 0.95 and recall on toxic class exceeds 80%, indicating accuracy was misleadingly low but model is genuinely strong.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Model achieves moderate recall (50-70%) but stakeholders accept the tradeoff given precision requirements.

must_not_claim: model is overfitting due to small training set
BERT is too large for this task
stratified split is incorrect

ground_truth_rationale: The core flaw is dual: both the metric (accuracy) and the baseline (majority-class) are trivially uninformative for an imbalanced detection task. The cost-sensitivity framing makes the metric mismatch even more severe. The three-seed consistency is genuine methodological rigor but irrelevant to the metric choice problem.
notes: Easy case: both flaws are direct and independently obvious. Intended to test whether evaluators flag both issues or only one.

task_prompt: A cybersecurity company trained an autoencoder-based anomaly detection model on network traffic logs to detect intrusions. The model is trained on 30 days of normal traffic (no labeled attacks) and evaluated on a held-out test set containing 28 days of normal traffic and 2 days of simulated attack traffic injected by a red team. The overall anomaly rate in the test set is 6.3%. They set the anomaly threshold at the 95th percentile of reconstruction errors on the training set. The model achieves Precision of 0.71 and Recall of 0.68 on the injected attack traffic. Their baseline is an Isolation Forest trained on the same data with default hyperparameters. The isolation forest achieves Precision of 0.59 and Recall of 0.54. The team claims a substantial improvement in intrusion detection capability. The paper notes that the red team was aware of the model's training window (they designed attacks for after the training cutoff) but was not aware of the specific features used by the model. The evaluation is conducted in an offline replay environment, not in live network traffic. The team plans to deploy the autoencoder in a real-time streaming environment with sub-second latency requirements. A pre-specified power analysis determined that 2 days of attack traffic (approximately 180 attack events) provides 85% power to detect a 15% recall improvement at alpha=0.05, which is why only 2 days of attack data were collected.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The autoencoder shows genuine improvement over the isolation forest baseline, but the simulated attack evaluation does not capture the adversarial adaptation that occurs in real deployments, and the offline-to-online gap for streaming anomaly detection is not addressed.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate on a blind red team exercise where attackers are not constrained by knowledge of the training window, and pilot in shadow mode on live traffic before production deployment.

planted_issues.description: Red team attacks designed after the training cutoff but without knowledge of model features do not represent adaptive adversaries who would probe and evade the specific model in production.
Offline replay evaluation on static logs does not capture the distributional shift and real-time feature availability that characterize streaming deployment environments.
Comparing against an isolation forest with default hyperparameters, rather than a tuned isolation forest, gives the autoencoder an unfair advantage.

ideal_critique: The isolation forest baseline uses default hyperparameters while the autoencoder's threshold is tuned to the 95th percentile; this is not an equitable comparison.
Simulated red team attacks designed with partial knowledge of the system are not representative of adaptive real-world adversaries.
Offline replay evaluation on static logs cannot account for real-time feature availability, streaming latency, or concept drift in live network traffic.
ideal_defense: The pre-specified power analysis for attack event count is a legitimate and rigorous methodological choice that should be credited.
Red team awareness of the training window but not of model features is a reasonable middle ground for attack simulation.
Training on unlabeled normal traffic is the operationally correct approach when labeled attack data is scarce.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Shadow-mode deployment on live traffic shows precision below 0.55 or recall below 0.50 due to distribution shift or adaptive adversarial behavior.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Shadow-mode deployment replicates offline metrics within 10 percentage points and the tuned isolation forest underperforms the autoencoder on the same data.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Shadow-mode results are within 15 points of offline for precision but show worse recall, indicating partial but not complete generalization.

must_not_claim: the training set is too small at 30 days
the 6.3% anomaly rate in the test set is too high
the pre-specified power analysis is incorrect or invalid

ground_truth_rationale: The power analysis for attack event count is explicitly valid (pre-specified, correctly computed) and is a red herring. The real issues are the tuning asymmetry between models and the offline-streaming gap. The correct_position is 'mixed' because the autoencoder likely does outperform the baseline, but the evaluation is insufficient to justify production deployment.
notes: Medium difficulty. The pre-specified power analysis (85% power, alpha=0.05) is prominently described and looks suspicious but is actually valid — planted as a red herring. Tests whether evaluators credit legitimate methodology.

task_prompt: A recommendation systems team at a streaming video platform developed a two-tower neural retrieval model to replace their existing collaborative filtering (CF) system. They evaluate both models using a standard leave-one-out protocol: for each user, the most recent interaction is held out as the test item, and the model must rank it among 99 randomly sampled negative items. The team reports Hit Rate@10 (HR@10) of 0.74 for their two-tower model versus 0.61 for the CF baseline, a 13-point improvement. The two-tower model additionally incorporates item content features (genre, description embeddings, runtime). The team argues that the content features are especially valuable for cold-start items, and they present a cold-start sub-analysis showing 0.58 HR@10 for new items versus 0.31 for CF. The negative sampling in the leave-one-out evaluation uses uniform random sampling over the full item catalog. The team notes that their model was trained on 8 months of interaction data and tested on the final held-out month. They acknowledge that production serving latency is a constraint and report that the two-tower model meets the 20ms P99 latency target. The team recommends full A/B test rollout based on these results, arguing that the large offline improvement justifies investment in the A/B infrastructure.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Random negative sampling in leave-one-out evaluation produces inflated and unreliable ranking metrics because it underrepresents hard negatives that appear in real ranked lists.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-evaluate using popularity-weighted negative sampling or full-catalog ranking, then assess whether the HR@10 gap persists.

planted_issues.description: Uniform random negative sampling over the full catalog produces an easy ranking task that inflates HR@10; real ranking must distinguish the target from popular items the user may also like.
The two-tower model has access to content features unavailable to the CF baseline, making it impossible to isolate the architectural contribution from the feature contribution.
The team proceeds to A/B recommendation based solely on offline HR@10, without acknowledging that offline-to-online correlation for retrieval metrics is often low in streaming contexts.

ideal_critique: Random negative sampling is well-known to produce overly optimistic ranking metrics; the sampled negatives are mostly clearly irrelevant items, not the hard negatives that matter in production.
The CF baseline does not have content features; comparing a pure CF model against a content-augmented neural model conflates two independent variables.
Justifying a full A/B test solely on offline HR@10 ignores the documented offline-online gap in recommendation systems.
ideal_defense: Leave-one-out with sampled negatives is a widely used protocol and allows comparison with published baselines.
Cold-start analysis is a legitimate and useful sub-evaluation that addresses a real deployment concern.
The latency constraint is correctly characterized and the 20ms compliance is a valid operational metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: HR@10 under popularity-weighted sampling shows the gap shrinks below 5 points, or the A/B test shows no statistically significant improvement in engagement.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Popularity-weighted sampling preserves the 13-point HR@10 gap and A/B test confirms engagement lift.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Popularity-weighted sampling reduces but does not eliminate the gap, leaving a moderate offline advantage that may or may not translate online.

must_not_claim: model is overfitting because training data is only 8 months
two-tower architecture is inappropriate for this task
latency target is too lenient

ground_truth_rationale: The dominant flaw is random negative sampling, which is widely known to inflate metrics relative to popularity-weighted or full-catalog evaluation. The content feature confound is a secondary but real issue that prevents clean attribution. The latency compliance is genuine and not a flaw.
notes: Medium difficulty: requires knowing that random vs. popularity-weighted negative sampling is a major methodological distinction in RecSys literature. The latency report looks suspicious but is actually valid.

task_prompt: A supply chain analytics team developed an LSTM-based demand forecasting model for a grocery retailer with 200 SKUs across 15 store locations. Training data spans 3 years of weekly sales, with the final 6 months used as a held-out test set. They compare the LSTM against a seasonal naive baseline, which predicts that demand in any week equals demand in the same week of the prior year. The team reports Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 12.3% for the LSTM versus 19.7% for the seasonal naive, a 37% relative improvement. They note that they carefully avoided any overlap between training and test windows, using a strict cutoff date. The team also reports that for 22 of the 200 SKUs, the LSTM's MAPE exceeded the seasonal naive baseline, but argues this is acceptable because the aggregate MAPE is much lower and those 22 SKUs are lower-volume items. The hyperparameters of the LSTM were tuned using Bayesian optimization on a validation set taken from months 25–30 of the training period. The paper does not include a comparison against an ARIMA or ETS model, arguing that the seasonal naive is the most commonly used operational baseline at this retailer. A footnote mentions that 14 of the 200 SKUs have intermittent demand (many zero-sales weeks), and these are included in the MAPE calculation.

ground_truth.final_verdict: MAPE is undefined or unreliable for intermittent demand SKUs with zero actual values, and excluding a statistical baseline (ARIMA/ETS) leaves open whether the LSTM's gain is over the simplest possible benchmark.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-compute MAPE excluding intermittent SKUs and compare against an ETS or ARIMA baseline; also report WAPE or RMSSE to handle zero-demand weeks.

planted_issues.description: MAPE is undefined when actual demand is zero and produces extreme values for near-zero weeks; including 14 intermittent SKUs in MAPE calculation distorts the aggregate metric.
Omitting an ARIMA or ETS baseline makes it impossible to determine whether the LSTM's improvement over seasonal naive is due to ML capability or simply better trend/seasonality decomposition.
The admission that 22 of 200 SKUs perform worse with the LSTM than the naive baseline deserves analysis; aggregated MAPE hides individual-SKU regressions that may matter operationally.

ideal_critique: MAPE with zero actual values (common in intermittent demand) is mathematically undefined or infinite; including these SKUs in an aggregate MAPE corrupts the comparison.
The seasonal naive is a weak operational benchmark; a well-specified ETS or ARIMA model is the appropriate statistical baseline for this type of comparison.
Dismissing 22 SKU-level regressions as 'low-volume' requires volume-weighted analysis to justify; aggregate MAPE may be improved by a few large SKUs while causing real operational issues for others.
ideal_defense: The strict temporal split is correct and shows careful attention to avoiding look-ahead bias.
Bayesian hyperparameter optimization on a separate validation window is a rigorous tuning approach.
Using the operational baseline (seasonal naive) as the comparator has real-world relevance for business stakeholders.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After excluding intermittent SKUs or using WAPE, the LSTM advantage falls below 20% relative improvement, or ETS matches LSTM within 3 percentage points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: WAPE (immune to zero denominators) shows a comparable 30%+ improvement and ETS falls between naive and LSTM.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Corrected metric shows 20-30% improvement and ETS underperforms LSTM by a meaningful margin, but operational SKU-level regressions are not fully characterized.

must_not_claim: LSTM is inappropriate for time series forecasting
6-month test set is too short
Bayesian optimization causes data leakage

ground_truth_rationale: The primary flaw is MAPE's behavior on zero-actual observations (intermittent demand), which is a well-known limitation in forecasting evaluation. The absence of a statistical baseline compounds this by making the comparison against an easily beaten heuristic. The Bayesian optimization on a separate validation window is valid methodology.
notes: Medium difficulty: requires knowing MAPE's zero-denominator pathology specifically in intermittent demand contexts, and awareness that seasonal naive is a weak baseline in forecasting benchmarks.

task_prompt: A hospital data science team developed a logistic regression model to predict 30-day readmission risk in patients discharged after heart failure hospitalization. The dataset includes 380 discharges over three years from a single tertiary care center, of which 61 resulted in readmission (16%). The model uses 14 clinical predictors: age, sex, ejection fraction, BNP at discharge, serum creatinine, sodium, hemoglobin, length of stay, number of prior admissions, diuretic dose, ACE inhibitor use, beta-blocker use, discharge disposition, and distance from hospital. The team reports an AUC of 0.76 on a 20% random holdout and compares this to the LACE index (a published 4-variable rule) which achieves AUC 0.64 on the same holdout. They conclude their model substantially outperforms the existing clinical decision rule. To demonstrate reliability, they report bootstrap confidence intervals (1000 resamples) on AUC: their model 0.76 (95% CI: 0.68–0.83), LACE index 0.64 (95% CI: 0.56–0.72). The paper notes that all 14 predictors were included based on clinical literature review, not data-driven selection. They also state that no interaction terms or penalization were used because the clinical team wanted coefficients that could be explained at the bedside. They intend to deploy this model in the discharge planning workflow to identify high-risk patients for care coordination. The team highlights that the bootstrap intervals show non-overlapping confidence intervals, claiming statistically meaningful superiority.

ground_truth.final_verdict: With only 61 readmission events and 14 predictors, the model's EPV of 4.4 is far below the clinical ML minimum of 10, making the reported AUC unreliable and the model likely overfit despite internal bootstrap CIs.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Reduce predictor count to no more than 6 (EPV ≥ 10), retrain, and compare AUC on an external validation cohort; alternatively, apply penalized regression (LASSO) and report calibration.

planted_issues.description: With 61 events and 14 predictors, EPV is 4.4, well below the 10-per-variable minimum accepted in clinical prediction modeling, rendering the model prone to overfitting and the AUC estimate unreliable.
A single-center random holdout is insufficient for clinical deployment; external validation on a different center or time period is required to establish generalizability.
AUC measures discrimination only; for clinical deployment, calibration (e.g., Hosmer-Lemeshow or calibration plot) must be reported to assess whether predicted probabilities are reliable.
Random 80/20 split on hospital encounter data ignores temporal autocorrelation; a temporal split would more honestly simulate prospective deployment.

ideal_critique: EPV of 4.4 violates a well-established clinical prediction modeling standard; this alone invalidates the reported AUC as a reliable estimate of out-of-sample performance.
Bootstrap confidence intervals quantify sampling variability but do not correct for overfitting caused by low EPV; they are not a substitute for external validation.
Calibration is absent; a model with AUC 0.76 but poor calibration would give clinicians systematically wrong risk probabilities.
Random split on a time-series clinical dataset introduces data leakage through shared temporal trends.
ideal_defense: Using all 14 clinically motivated predictors is a reasonable starting point; variable selection based on clinical theory is methodologically defensible.
The bootstrap CI computation is correctly executed and the non-overlap of intervals does indicate a discrimination difference.
Comparing to LACE on the same holdout is a fair apples-to-apples comparison of discrimination.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: External validation AUC drops below 0.68 or EPV-corrected AUC (using optimism correction via bootstrapping) closes the gap with LACE to under 5 points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: External validation at a second center yields AUC above 0.73 with good calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p > 0.05).
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: External validation AUC is 0.69-0.72 with moderate calibration, suggesting some generalizability but not enough for confident clinical deployment.

must_not_claim: logistic regression is too simple for this task
AUC of 0.76 is too low to be clinically useful
bootstrap resampling is invalid

ground_truth_rationale: The dominant flaw requiring domain-specific knowledge is the EPV violation: 14 variables with 61 events yields EPV = 4.4, far below the clinical standard of 10. This is not flagged by general ML intuition but is a fundamental rule in clinical prediction modeling (Harrell, Peduzzi). The bootstrap CIs are correctly computed but misleading because they do not correct for model overfit.
notes: Hard case requiring clinical prediction modeling domain knowledge (EPV rule). General ML practitioners will see the small n but typically won't quantify EPV or know the threshold.

task_prompt: A team at a legal tech company developed a transformer-based contract clause classifier to identify 22 clause types (e.g., indemnification, governing law, limitation of liability) in commercial contracts. They fine-tuned a domain-adapted legal BERT model (Legal-BERT) on a dataset of 3,800 contracts with clause-level annotations produced by two junior paralegals. The team reports macro-averaged F1 of 0.81 on a random 15% holdout, compared to a TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline achieving macro-F1 of 0.71. They argue the 10-point improvement justifies replacing the existing rule-based clause extraction system. The paper notes that the two paralegals had a Cohen's kappa of 0.63 on the annotation task. The team's training procedure merged both annotators' labels using majority vote (which, for two annotators, resolves to annotator 1's label whenever there is disagreement). The paper presents learning curves showing that model performance plateaus around 3,000 training examples, suggesting the dataset is large enough. The contracts came from three business sectors—software, procurement, and real estate—and the team states that sector-specific terminology was left in place to preserve realism. The team evaluates across all 22 clause types but notes that 7 of the 22 types have fewer than 30 examples in the test set, and the macro-F1 average weights all clause types equally.

ground_truth.final_verdict: A Cohen's kappa of 0.63 represents moderate inter-annotator agreement that is below the 0.70-0.80 threshold conventionally required for reliable NLP annotation, and majority vote with two annotators is not majority voting — it is simply defaulting to annotator 1 on all disagreements, introducing systematic annotator bias.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-adjudicate all 37% disagreement cases using a senior attorney and recompute kappa; separately evaluate model performance on clauses where both annotators agreed versus disagreed to measure the effect of annotation noise.

planted_issues.description: Cohen's kappa of 0.63 is below the accepted NLP annotation reliability threshold of 0.70-0.80; model performance on low-agreement labels is likely overestimated.
Majority vote with two annotators resolves to annotator 1's label on all disagreements; this is not a noise-reduction technique but annotation by a single annotator with selective review.

Equal weighting in macro-F1 gives the same influence to clause types with fewer than 30 test examples as to those with hundreds; these rare-type scores are highly variable and dominate variance in the aggregate.
If the random holdout splits at clause level rather than contract level, clauses from the same contract appear in both train and test, causing document-level data leakage.

ideal_critique: Kappa of 0.63 is moderate agreement; the NLP annotation community typically requires kappa above 0.7 (and ideally above 0.8) for training high-quality classifiers.
The two-annotator majority vote procedure is algebraically equivalent to using annotator 1's label whenever there is disagreement — this is not adjudication.
Macro-F1 with 7 rare-class clause types (n<30) is dominated by noise; a micro-F1 or frequency-weighted macro-F1 would be more informative.
Clause-level splits on contract-derived data risk leaking document-level style and context across train and test.
ideal_defense: Legal-BERT fine-tuning is appropriate for legal text classification and is a well-justified architectural choice.
Learning curves showing plateau at 3,000 examples provide genuine evidence that data quantity is not the primary bottleneck.
The 10-point macro-F1 improvement over TF-IDF logistic regression is potentially meaningful even with annotation noise, as the noise affects both models equally.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Reannotation by a senior attorney reveals annotator 1 was systematically wrong on specific clause types, and model F1 drops more than 5 points on reannotated data.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Senior attorney adjudication produces kappa above 0.78 with both paralegals and the relabeled model achieves F1 within 2 points of original report.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Adjudication improves kappa to 0.71 and the model performance drops 3-4 points, which is notable but not disqualifying for the classification task.

must_not_claim: Legal-BERT is inappropriate for contract classification
3800 contracts is too few examples to fine-tune
learning curve analysis is invalid

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires NLP annotation standards knowledge: the kappa threshold (0.7-0.8 for reliable annotation) and the two-annotator majority-vote fallacy are both domain-specific norms that general ML practitioners may not recognize. The learning curve analysis is actually valid evidence and is a planted red herring — it addresses a different concern (data quantity) than annotation quality.
notes: Hard case requiring NLP annotation best practices. The learning curve plateau is a red herring that looks like evidence of robustness but doesn't address annotation quality.

task_prompt: A music streaming platform team developed a reinforcement learning-based playlist continuation model to improve long-session engagement. They train using offline RL (behavior cloning with advantage weighting) on six months of user listening logs. To evaluate, they compare against their current production greedy recommendation policy using a counterfactual evaluation framework: inverse propensity scoring (IPS) is applied to the logged data to estimate what engagement metrics would have been under the new policy. The IPS estimator uses propensity scores estimated from the logging policy's action probabilities, which are derived from the production system's internal scoring model. The team reports a statistically significant 4.2% IPS-estimated improvement in session length and a 3.1% improvement in skip rate, both with bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals that exclude zero. They argue that IPS-based counterfactual evaluation is best practice and avoids the need for an A/B test before deployment. The team notes that the music catalog contains 80 million tracks but the logging policy had non-negligible probability estimates only for a very small subset of tracks per context (effective support of approximately 200-400 tracks per query). They further note that the new RL policy is designed to surface long-tail tracks and can rank songs that were almost never shown historically under the production policy. They plan to deploy the RL model, which can recommend any of the 80 million tracks, without a live A/B test.

ground_truth.final_verdict: IPS counterfactual evaluation is invalid here because the new RL policy can recommend tracks outside the effective support of the logging policy, violating the coverage assumption required for unbiased IPS estimation.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a live A/B test, or at minimum restrict evaluation to contexts where the new policy's recommendations overlap with the logging policy's support and report the fraction of queries meeting this criterion.

planted_issues.description: IPS counterfactual evaluation assumes the logging policy has non-zero probability of taking any action the evaluation policy would take; with 80M tracks but only 200-400 in the logging policy's support, this assumption is massively violated for most RL recommendations.
Propensity scores for tracks outside the logging policy's support are undefined or set to near-zero, causing IPS weights to be numerically unstable or require clipping that biases the estimator.
Offline RL trained on the logging policy's behavior distribution may systematically overestimate reward for out-of-distribution actions that were rarely or never taken by the production system.

ideal_critique: IPS requires that every action the target policy takes has non-zero probability under the logging policy; with a catalog of 80M tracks and an effective logging support of ~300, the vast majority of RL recommendations cannot be evaluated without extrapolation.
Propensity scores of near-zero produce very large IPS weights that require clipping; any clipping introduces bias and the reported CIs no longer reflect the true variance.
Offline RL is especially susceptible to distribution shift because the value function is trained on a restricted action space but evaluated (or deployed) in a much larger space.
ideal_defense: IPS-based counterfactual evaluation is an accepted best practice in recommendation systems research when a full A/B test is not feasible.
Bootstrapped confidence intervals correctly characterize sampling uncertainty in the IPS estimate given the observed data.
Behavior cloning with advantage weighting is a reasonable conservative offline RL approach that stays closer to the logging policy than model-free RL.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The fraction of RL recommendations within the logging policy's effective support is below 30%, confirming that most IPS estimates are extrapolated.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A live A/B test confirms a 3%+ improvement in session length, retroactively validating the offline estimate despite the coverage concern.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The RL policy's effective recommendations overlap substantially with the logging policy's support (>70%) due to convergence toward popular tracks, reducing but not eliminating the coverage concern.

must_not_claim: offline RL is an inappropriate algorithm for this task
six months of training data is insufficient
bootstrapped CIs are invalid for IPS estimates

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires knowledge of the coverage/overlap assumption in off-policy evaluation (a core theoretical requirement for unbiased IPS estimation). The team's framing sounds correct ('IPS is best practice') but the 80M-track catalog with 200-400 track support per query makes the coverage assumption catastrophically violated. Behavior cloning with advantage weighting is actually a valid conservative offline RL approach.
notes: Hard case requiring recommendation systems / counterfactual evaluation domain knowledge. The 'IPS is best practice' framing is technically true in general but inapplicable here due to coverage violation.

task_prompt: A bank's machine learning team developed a new fraud detection model and evaluated it using ROC-AUC. They report an AUC of 0.97 and claim this demonstrates near-perfect fraud detection capability. The dataset contains 500,000 transactions, of which 1,200 (0.24%) are fraudulent. The team uses AUC as their primary evaluation metric because it is 'threshold-independent' and is widely reported in the fraud detection literature. Their deployment plan sets a fixed alert threshold that generates alerts for 0.5% of all transactions. At this threshold, the model catches 61% of fraud cases (730 of 1,200). The bank's fraud operations team processes approximately 2,500 alerts per day. The paper does not report precision at the deployment threshold. The team notes that they carefully constructed the evaluation using a stratified sample to ensure the 0.24% fraud prevalence is preserved in the test set. They also note that alerts must be reviewed within minutes because many flagged transactions are card-present purchases occurring in real time. Fraud investigators are staffed for a fixed review queue each shift, so the value of the model depends on how many reviewed alerts are actually fraudulent, not just on rank ordering. The model is proposed to replace the current rule-based system, which catches 55% of fraud at the same false positive rate. The team competes for internal investment based on the AUC of 0.97.

ground_truth.final_verdict: AUC of 0.97 on a 0.24% prevalence dataset is misleading because it is dominated by true negative performance; precision at the deployment threshold (approximately 4.9%) is the operationally relevant metric and is not reported.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report Precision-Recall AUC, precision at the operating threshold, and cost-based evaluation incorporating fraud amount recovered versus alert handling cost.

planted_issues.description: With 0.24% fraud prevalence, ROC-AUC is dominated by true negative performance and does not reflect the model's ability to rank fraud cases; Precision-Recall AUC is more informative for extreme class imbalance.

The team does not report precision at the deployment threshold despite setting a fixed operational threshold; at 0.5% alert rate with 61% recall, precision is approximately 4.9%, meaning 95% of alerts are false positives.

ideal_critique: ROC-AUC of 0.97 on a 0.24% fraud dataset is expected to be high regardless of actual fraud-ranking quality because the vast majority of cases are non-fraud and trivially correctly classified.
The operational metric is precision at the deployment threshold; approximately 95% of the 2,500 daily alerts would be false positives, which is a significant operational burden that AUC conceals.
Precision-Recall AUC or Average Precision is the appropriate metric for severely imbalanced binary classification.
ideal_defense: The model does catch 61% of fraud versus 55% for the rule-based system, representing a genuine 6-point recall improvement at the same alert rate.
Stratified test set construction correctly preserves the realistic class prevalence in evaluation.
AUC is widely used and reported in fraud detection papers, allowing comparison with published benchmarks.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Precision-Recall AUC shows a much smaller gap between the new model and the rule-based system, or cost-benefit analysis shows the marginal alert-handling cost exceeds the recovered fraud value.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The 6-point recall gain at the same alert rate is confirmed as statistically significant and translates to meaningful fraud dollar recovery that exceeds operational costs.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The recall improvement is real but the cost-benefit calculation depends on fraud dollar amounts and analyst salaries that vary by institution.

must_not_claim: model is overfitting because AUC is too high
stratified sampling is incorrect
0.24% fraud rate is too rare to model

ground_truth_rationale: ROC-AUC on extreme class imbalance is a well-known metric mismatch; the correct metric is Precision-Recall AUC or Average Precision. The 6-point recall improvement is real, making this a 'mixed' case — the model is genuinely better, but AUC overstates the magnitude and hides the precision reality. Stratified sampling is valid methodology.
notes: Easy case. The metric mismatch (AUC on severe imbalance) is widely known and directly observable from the reported numbers. Intended to test basic metric literacy.

task_prompt: A team at a retail company built a gradient boosting model to predict whether a customer will respond to a promotional email campaign. They define 'response' as clicking any link in the email within 7 days. The training data contains 200,000 customers from the previous six monthly campaigns. The model achieves AUC of 0.79. The team proposes to use the model to select the top 10% of customers most likely to respond for targeting in the next campaign. The team reports overall accuracy of 81% on the test set, noting that the response rate is 18%. They compare to a random targeting strategy and claim the ML approach outperforms random selection by a wide margin on accuracy. The team did not report lift, gain curves, or cumulative response rate among the top-10% decile. The email program has fixed creative costs and a marginal send cost, so the business decision depends on concentrating responses in the targeted decile rather than improving average classification performance across all customers. In past campaigns, only the top portion of the customer list was ever contacted because of budget and fatigue constraints. The marketing manager asks the team: 'Will targeting the top 10% predicted customers improve our campaign ROI?' The team answers yes, pointing to the 79% AUC and 81% accuracy figures.

ground_truth.final_verdict: For a targeting problem selecting the top 10% of customers, the operational metric is decile lift or cumulative gain among the top decile, not overall accuracy or AUC; without these metrics, the team cannot answer the marketing manager's question.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compute lift chart and cumulative gain curve; report the response rate among the top 10% predicted customers versus the overall 18% baseline rate.

planted_issues.description: Overall accuracy measures performance across all 200,000 customers, but the deployment selects only the top 10%; accuracy does not describe the model's ability to concentrate responders in the selected group.

For a campaign targeting problem, decile lift (or top-10% response rate divided by baseline rate) is the directly relevant operational metric and is not reported despite being straightforward to compute.

ideal_critique: Overall accuracy on an 18/82 split is dominated by correctly classifying non-responders; a model could achieve 82% accuracy by predicting everyone as non-responsive.
The operational question — will the top 10% contain more responders? — is answered by lift or cumulative gain, not by AUC or accuracy.
Without reporting the response rate among the top-10% predicted group, the team has not answered the marketing manager's question.
ideal_defense: AUC of 0.79 does indicate meaningful discrimination and is a legitimate model quality indicator.
The training data covers six months of campaigns, providing sufficient volume and temporal diversity.
Gradient boosting is an appropriate algorithm for tabular propensity scoring.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The top-10% decile response rate is below 25% (less than 1.4x lift), indicating the model provides minimal targeting advantage.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The top-10% decile response rate exceeds 35% (2x lift), confirming the model is a useful targeting tool despite the metric reporting gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Lift is 1.5-2x, indicating some targeting value but not enough to definitively resolve the marketing ROI question without cost data.

must_not_claim: gradient boosting is overfitting on tabular data
AUC of 0.79 is too low
the 18% response rate is too low to model

ground_truth_rationale: The metric mismatch is direct: overall accuracy and AUC are model quality metrics that do not answer the operational question of decile-level response concentration. Lift and cumulative gain charts are the standard tools for this use case. This is an easy case because the mismatch is explicit in the story structure.
notes: Easy metric mismatch. Tests whether evaluators distinguish model quality metrics (AUC, accuracy) from operational targeting metrics (lift, cumulative gain).

task_prompt: A team at a financial services company built an extractive question answering model to help compliance officers find relevant passages in regulatory documents. Given a question like 'What are the reporting requirements for transactions exceeding $10,000?' the model highlights the most relevant passage from the relevant regulation. They evaluate on an internal benchmark of 340 question-passage pairs manually curated by the compliance team, using Exact Match (EM) and F1 token overlap (as used in the SQuAD benchmark). The model achieves EM of 0.68 and F1 of 0.79. The compliance team baseline (manually searching the document) achieves an EM of 0.91 and F1 of 0.92 when given unlimited time. The team argues that the model's EM of 0.68 represents acceptable performance for a first version since it automates the process and frees up compliance officer time. They also note that response latency is 0.3 seconds versus 4-8 minutes for manual search. The deployment plan keeps a human reviewer in the loop, but the product team wants to measure success mainly by reduced search time rather than by exact legal sufficiency of the returned passage. The team is planning to deploy the system with compliance officers reviewing all model outputs before acting. The paper does not report whether the evaluation queries are representative of real compliance officer questions or whether the questions were written knowing the answers in advance.

ground_truth.final_verdict: EM and F1 token overlap are appropriate metrics for extractive QA but the evaluation benchmark may have construct validity problems: compliance officer questions written by the same team that built the model are likely easier than real-world questions, and EM does not account for legally equivalent paraphrases.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a prospective evaluation where compliance officers submit real queries during normal work and rate the relevance of retrieved passages on a 5-point scale; also measure task completion time with and without the model.

planted_issues.description: The evaluation benchmark was curated by the compliance team building the model, creating potential for inadvertent in-distribution bias where questions are easier than real-world queries.

EM requires character-level match with the reference answer; legally equivalent paraphrases or different citation formats would score zero EM even if the extracted passage correctly answers the compliance question.

Comparing the model to compliance officers with unlimited search time is an unfair human baseline; the relevant comparison is model-assisted versus unassisted compliance officer performance under realistic time constraints.

ideal_critique: Questions curated by the model-building team are likely biased toward queries the model was implicitly designed to answer; an independent blind test set is required.
Exact Match in a legal compliance context is too strict for paraphrase-rich regulatory language and too forgiving when a model extracts the correct paragraph but not the exact cited sentence.
The human baseline of 'unlimited time' does not reflect realistic compliance officer workloads; the comparison should be timed realistic task completion.
ideal_defense: The compliance team's involvement in benchmark creation also means they know what constitutes a valid answer; this is not purely a conflict of interest.
F1 token overlap partially addresses EM's strictness by measuring partial credit for overlapping tokens.
The speed improvement (0.3s vs. 4-8 minutes) is a real operational benefit that is correctly characterized.
Human-in-the-loop review before acting is an appropriate risk management strategy for a first version.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Prospective evaluation with real compliance queries shows passage relevance ratings below 3.5 out of 5, or query difficulty is significantly higher than benchmark queries.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Prospective evaluation replicates benchmark-level performance with an average relevance rating above 4 out of 5 on real compliance queries.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Prospective evaluation shows EM of 0.55-0.65 on real queries (below benchmark), indicating some generalization gap but the system still provides value given the speed advantage.

must_not_claim: extractive QA is wrong approach for regulatory documents
EM of 0.68 is too low for any deployment
0.3 second latency is inadequate

ground_truth_rationale: The correct position is 'mixed': the metrics (EM, F1) are standard and appropriate for extractive QA, but the benchmark has construct validity concerns that are harder to detect. The human-in-the-loop review is a genuine risk mitigation. The team's argument that EM of 0.68 is acceptable for a first version is defensible given the speed advantage and human review requirement.
notes: Medium difficulty mixed case. The 0.3-second latency and human-in-the-loop review are valid features that a critique should not attack. Tests whether evaluators distinguish benchmark validity concerns from metric choice problems.

task_prompt: A news personalization team at a digital media company retrained their article recommendation model using a new user interest embedding approach. They evaluated using NDCG@10 on a static held-out test set constructed from historical click data. The new model achieves NDCG@10 of 0.412 versus 0.381 for the current production model, an 8.1% relative improvement. The team argues that NDCG@10 is the industry standard for evaluating ranked recommendation lists and that an 8.1% improvement on this metric justifies full production rollout across their 40 million user base. The evaluation test set was sampled from the same three-month period as the training data, with a two-week holdout at the end. The team notes that click-through data was used to infer relevance labels: any article a user clicked is labeled relevant, and all others are labeled irrelevant. They also note that the homepage layout and story mix were changing frequently during that quarter because of a major election cycle, but the evaluation used the historical logs as recorded. The team reports that their model generates more diverse article recommendations (intra-list diversity metric improved 14%), and they argue this further supports deployment. They acknowledge that the new model may recommend more niche articles, which could affect user engagement in ways not captured by NDCG.

ground_truth.final_verdict: NDCG@10 computed on click-derived static labels does not measure the causal effect on user engagement and fails to account for position bias in historical click data, making the offline improvement unreliable as a predictor of online engagement.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct an A/B test measuring session-level engagement metrics (session length, return rate, article completion rate) and apply position bias correction (e.g., inverse propensity weighting) to the offline evaluation.

planted_issues.description: Clicks used as relevance labels contain position bias: articles shown in top positions receive more clicks regardless of true relevance, inflating NDCG for models that replicate historical position patterns.

NDCG on static historical clicks is poorly correlated with online engagement metrics in news recommendation; more diverse recommendations may hurt short-term clicks even if they improve long-term retention.
Binary click/no-click labels do not capture reading completion, sharing, or return visits, which are more meaningful engagement signals for a news platform.

ideal_critique: NDCG computed on click-labeled static data reflects the logging policy's position biases, not true user preferences; the new model may be rewarded for replicating historical biases.
Offline-to-online correlation for NDCG is consistently low in news recommendation, where novelty, recency, and diversity effects dominate user behavior.
The team's own observation that the model 'may recommend more niche articles' suggests it may sacrifice click-optimized positions for diversity, which NDCG would not correctly account for.
ideal_defense: NDCG@10 is a principled ranking metric and is widely used in academic and industry recommendation benchmarks.
The two-week holdout period provides temporal separation between training and evaluation, which is methodologically sound.
Reporting intra-list diversity alongside NDCG shows awareness of beyond-accuracy metrics.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A/B test shows no statistically significant improvement in session length or return rate, or shows regression in click-through rate on niche articles.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A/B test shows significant improvement in session length and return rate despite comparable or slightly lower raw CTR.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A/B test shows CTR improvement but no change in session-level engagement, leaving unclear whether the offline NDCG gain is meaningful.

must_not_claim: NDCG is not a valid metric for ranked lists
model is overfitting to training data
two-week holdout is insufficient temporal separation

ground_truth_rationale: This is an offline-to-online gap case: NDCG on static click labels is an industry-standard metric that is nonetheless poorly correlated with online news engagement, particularly when diversity changes. Position bias in click labels is a well-documented confound. The diversity improvement is genuine and potentially valuable but not measured by NDCG.
notes: Medium difficulty offline-to-online gap case. Tests whether evaluators know that NDCG on biased click data is not equivalent to unbiased relevance measurement.

task_prompt: A large e-commerce platform team developed a new product ranking model specifically aimed at improving long-term customer retention and repeat purchase rate. They frame this as a 'discovery model' — designed to surface relevant but novel products to encourage customers to explore new categories rather than just buy items similar to their purchase history. The team evaluates this model using standard offline metrics: Precision@5 and NDCG@10 computed on a test set of historical purchase data. The new model achieves Precision@5 of 0.31 and NDCG@10 of 0.44, compared to 0.34 and 0.47 for the current production model. The discovery model performs slightly worse on both offline metrics, which the team acknowledges but argues is expected: the model is designed to surface novel products that customers have not interacted with yet, and therefore historical interactions cannot serve as ground truth for novelty-oriented recommendations. The team proposes deployment based on two supporting analyses: first, a catalog coverage metric shows the discovery model recommends items from 3.2x more product categories than the production model; second, a user study with 120 participants found that 67% preferred discovery model recommendations when shown side-by-side. The platform has 18 million monthly active users and a long-term retention rate (12-month repeat purchase) of 31%.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The team correctly identifies that historical-purchase-based Precision and NDCG cannot evaluate a novelty-oriented model, but the supporting evidence (catalog coverage and a 120-person user study) is insufficient to justify deployment to 18 million users; a properly powered A/B test on long-term retention is required.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Design an A/B test powered to detect a 1-2 percentage point change in 12-month repeat purchase rate; run for at least 6 months to capture the retention signal; also measure 30-day and 90-day return visit rate as leading indicators.

planted_issues.description: Precision@5 and NDCG@10 based on historical purchases cannot evaluate a discovery model by design; items the model is supposed to surface have, by definition, not been purchased yet and would score as false negatives.
A 120-person preference study measures immediate subjective preference, not 12-month retention; the team's stated goal (long-term retention) requires a powered longitudinal study or a large A/B test.

Catalog coverage (3.2x more product categories) measures diversity, not retention impact; wider exploration may increase returns, decrease them, or have no effect — coverage alone cannot justify the deployment claim.

ideal_critique: Precision@5 and NDCG on historical data are known to be invalid for evaluating novelty or serendipity-oriented models; this is well-established in RecSys literature, but using them as primary metrics while arguing they don't apply is internally contradictory.
A 120-person user preference study is severely underpowered to detect a 1-2 pp change in 12-month retention across 18M users, even if preference is a leading indicator.
Catalog coverage measures output diversity but not outcomes; higher diversity can harm retention if users are confused or presented with irrelevant novel items.
ideal_defense: The team correctly articulates why historical-purchase-based metrics are invalid for their model objective — this is sophisticated and technically correct.
User preference studies are a legitimate supplement to offline metrics and the 67% preference rate is notably strong.
Acknowledging the offline metric drop rather than hiding it is methodologically honest.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A 6-month A/B test shows no significant improvement in 30-day return visit rate or 90-day repeat purchase rate, indicating that diversity does not translate to retention.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A/B test shows significant improvement in 90-day repeat purchase rate (2+ pp lift) confirming that the discovery model achieves its design goal.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A/B test shows short-term return visit improvement (30-day) but no statistically significant 12-month retention signal within the study window.

must_not_claim: discovery models cannot improve retention
NDCG@10 drop means the model is worse
user studies are invalid for recommendation evaluation

ground_truth_rationale: The correct_position is 'mixed': the team is right that offline purchase-based metrics are inappropriate for a novelty model, but wrong to proceed to deployment without a powered A/B test. The user study preference result (67%) looks like strong evidence but is not powered for the retention hypothesis. This requires RecSys domain knowledge about offline metric validity for novelty-oriented models.
notes: Hard case. The team's argument about offline metric invalidity is technically correct, making this a mixed case where critique and defense both have valid points. Tests whether evaluators can simultaneously credit valid reasoning and identify insufficient evidence.

task_prompt: A quantitative finance team at an asset management firm developed a machine learning model to predict next-day returns for a universe of 500 large-cap equities. The model uses a gradient boosting approach with features derived from technical indicators, fundamental data, and alternative data (satellite imagery of retail parking lots, credit card transaction data). The team evaluates the model using Information Coefficient (IC), which is the Spearman rank correlation between model predictions and realized next-day returns, measured daily over a 12-month backtest period. They report mean IC of 0.07 and IC information ratio (ICIR) of 1.42 over the backtest. The team argues this is strong performance by industry standards (IC > 0.05 is typically considered actionable, ICIR > 1.0 indicates consistency). They propose to use the model's scores as alpha signals in a long-short portfolio strategy. The backtest does not include transaction costs, market impact, or slippage. A pre-specified power analysis determined that 252 trading days (1 year) provides 89% power to detect IC of 0.05 with ICIR > 1.0 at alpha=0.05, which is why the backtest is exactly 1 year. The team's backtest does not account for the survivorship bias in the equity universe, which is constructed using the current S&P 500 constituent list.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Survivorship bias from using the current S&P 500 constituent list significantly inflates IC because companies that were delisted or dropped from the index during the backtest period (often after poor performance) are excluded from the historical evaluation.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Reconstruct the backtest using point-in-time constituent data so that the equity universe at each historical date reflects the actual index composition at that date; recompute IC and ICIR on the survivorship-bias-corrected backtest.

planted_issues.description: Using current S&P 500 constituents for historical backtesting excludes companies that were delisted or removed from the index during the backtest period, introducing survivorship bias that inflates IC.
IC measures signal quality without accounting for transaction costs, slippage, or market impact; a model with IC 0.07 may generate no net alpha after implementation costs for a large AUM portfolio.

A 12-month backtest may span a single market regime; IC and ICIR are known to vary substantially across different volatility regimes and the model's out-of-sample consistency over multiple market cycles is untested.

ideal_critique: Survivorship bias from using the current S&P 500 list is a critical flaw in quantitative finance backtesting; companies that failed or were delisted are excluded, making the backtest universe cherry-picked.
IC measures signal before costs; with large-cap equities and 500 positions, daily rebalancing costs can easily consume an IC of 0.07.
One year of backtest may represent a single bull market regime; ICIR stability across different market environments (2008, 2020) would be required for confidence in the signal.
ideal_defense: Mean IC of 0.07 and ICIR of 1.42 are above industry thresholds that are genuinely considered meaningful in quantitative equity research.
The pre-specified power analysis for the backtest length is a legitimate methodological decision that should be credited.
Using gradient boosting with alternative data sources is a reasonable contemporary approach to equity signal generation.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Survivorship-bias-corrected IC drops below 0.04, or transaction-cost-adjusted IC is negative, or ICIR falls below 1.0 on a corrected backtest.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Point-in-time constituent data produces IC within 0.01 of the reported 0.07 and ICIR remains above 1.0, suggesting survivorship bias is small in this universe.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Corrected IC is 0.04-0.06 (below the 0.05 threshold but non-trivial), leaving the signal actionability question open pending transaction cost analysis.

must_not_claim: gradient boosting is inappropriate for return prediction
IC is the wrong metric for equity signal evaluation
the pre-specified power analysis is invalid or insufficient

ground_truth_rationale: Survivorship bias in equity backtesting is a well-known quantitative finance issue requiring point-in-time data. The pre-specified power analysis is a legitimate methodological feature that must not be criticized. Transaction cost omission compounds the IC inflation from survivorship bias. These require financial backtesting domain knowledge rather than general ML intuition.
notes: Medium difficulty with financial domain knowledge requirement. The power analysis is a deliberate valid red herring. Tests whether evaluators know the survivorship bias issue specific to S&P 500 constituent backtesting.

task_prompt: A hospital system developed a machine learning model to predict which ICU patients are likely to deteriorate in the next 4 hours, with the intended use of triggering a rapid response team (RRT) alert. The model is evaluated on a retrospective cohort of 8,200 ICU stays. The team reports AUC of 0.84 and claims this indicates strong predictive performance. The model was trained to predict a composite outcome label (in-hospital mortality OR transfer to higher-level care within 4 hours). The team compares their model to the Modified Early Warning Score (MEWS), a validated clinical scoring system, which achieves AUC 0.78 on the same cohort. They conclude that the ML model is clinically superior. To highlight the model's practical value, they note that at a threshold achieving 80% sensitivity, the model has a specificity of 62%. This means 38% of non-deteriorating patients would receive an RRT alert. The ICU has approximately 110 occupied beds on a typical day, and the team does not estimate how many additional alerts this threshold would generate per shift. The team argues that in ICU settings, false positives are low-cost because the RRT can quickly determine the patient is stable. The paper is submitted to a clinical informatics journal with the claim that the AUC improvement demonstrates clinical utility.

ground_truth.final_verdict: AUC is an inappropriate primary metric for claiming clinical utility in a high-alert-fatigue environment; the reported specificity of 62% at 80% sensitivity implies an alert fatigue burden that AUC conceals, and clinical utility requires decision-analytic evaluation (e.g., net benefit analysis or alert-fatigue-adjusted metrics).
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a decision curve analysis (DCA) comparing ML model net benefit to MEWS and to treat-all and treat-none strategies; report alert-fatigue-adjusted metrics including positive predictive value, alerts-per-event, and number needed to alert.

planted_issues.description: AUC summarizes performance across all thresholds and does not reflect the clinical tradeoff at any operational threshold; clinical utility requires decision-analytic frameworks such as DCA or net benefit.

38% false positive rate at the operating threshold in an ICU generates a high number of false RRT activations per shift; this alert fatigue burden is clinically significant and is not quantified or addressed.

The model is trained on a composite outcome (mortality OR transfer) but the clinical intervention (RRT alert) is designed to prevent deterioration leading to mortality; transfer may include planned transfers, diluting the outcome label.
Retrospective AUC on historical data does not account for clinical workflow integration effects; a prospective pilot is required before claiming clinical utility.

ideal_critique: AUC is a discrimination metric that does not tell clinicians whether the model is actually useful at any deployment threshold; decision curve analysis is the standard for clinical utility claims.

The team itself reports 38% false positive rate at the operational threshold; in a busy ICU, this translates to many unnecessary RRT activations per day, causing alert fatigue — a well-documented patient safety concern.
A composite outcome including elective transfers conflates distinct clinical events with different implications for RRT response.
Retrospective evaluation proves discrimination, not utility; prospective deployment may reveal workflow issues invisible in historical data.
ideal_defense: AUC of 0.84 does represent meaningful discrimination above MEWS (0.78), which is a validated and widely used clinical score.
80% sensitivity is a reasonable clinical requirement for a deterioration alert system; missing 20% of deterioration events has clinical consequences.
The team acknowledges the tradeoff explicitly rather than hiding the false positive rate.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Decision curve analysis shows the ML model has no net benefit over MEWS or treat-all strategy at clinically relevant threshold probabilities.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: DCA shows positive net benefit over MEWS across a range of threshold probabilities (1-10%), and alert rate per shift is below a pre-specified fatigue threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: DCA shows marginal net benefit but the alert fatigue burden (alerts per deterioration event) exceeds published RRT fatigue thresholds.

must_not_claim: AUC of 0.84 is too low for clinical use
MEWS is not a valid baseline
machine learning cannot outperform clinical scores in ICU settings

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires clinical ML domain knowledge: the standard for clinical utility claims is decision curve analysis (DCA), not AUC. The alert fatigue literature is a specific clinical concern that general ML practitioners may not know. The AUC improvement is real but insufficient to establish clinical utility — a distinction that is domain-specific.
notes: Hard case requiring clinical informatics domain knowledge: DCA, net benefit, and alert fatigue quantification are required for clinical utility claims in clinical informatics journals.

task_prompt: A subscription streaming service trained a gradient-boosted tree model to predict which users would cancel within the next 30 days. Training data covers January through October of a given year; the holdout test set covers November and December of the same year. The model achieved 0.81 AUC on the test set, substantially better than the 0.67 AUC of the previous rule-based system. The feature set includes days since last login, number of titles watched in the past 14 days, number of support tickets opened in the past 60 days, account age in months, plan tier (basic/standard/premium), and whether the user has a linked family sub-account. The streaming library consists of approximately 40,000 titles, and the recommendation engine updates personalization vectors nightly; the team notes that recommendation quality has improved over the same period, which may independently affect engagement. The product team reports that November–December is the period when the service runs its largest promotional campaign offering discounted annual plans, and that a substantial fraction of users who would otherwise churn instead upgrade or freeze their accounts during this window. Historically, churn rates in November–December are roughly 35% lower than the annual average across all other months. The data scientists argue the 0.81 AUC demonstrates that the new model is ready for production and should replace the rule-based system immediately. A reviewer notes that the test window coincides exactly with the promotional period and questions whether the performance estimate is representative of the model's year-round behavior.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The test AUC is inflated by seasonal confounding because the evaluation window coincides with a promotional campaign that systematically suppresses churn, making the estimate unrepresentative of year-round model performance.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-evaluate model AUC on held-out months from outside the promotional window (e.g., January–October) using a time-based split, and compare to the November–December AUC.

planted_issues.description: The test set spans the annual promotional campaign window, during which churn rates are abnormally suppressed, biasing the AUC estimate upward relative to non-campaign months.
Performance was assessed on a single two-month window with no cross-validation or multi-period holdout, making the estimate high-variance and time-specific.

ideal_critique: The November–December test window coincides with the service's largest promotional campaign, which actively converts churners into upgraders or account-freezers; this structural intervention suppresses the base churn rate and likely changes the feature-label relationship, making the AUC non-representative of typical months.

A model that is merely decent at ranking users by engagement activity will appear artificially strong during a period when high-risk users are being removed from the churn pool by external promotion, not by any genuine model-detectable signal.
The comparison to the 0.67 AUC rule-based system is also confounded if that system was evaluated on different months; without a common evaluation period the improvement claim is uninterpretable.
Production deployment decisions should rely on AUC measured across a diverse range of months including high-churn, low-churn, and mid-campaign periods.
ideal_defense: The promotional campaign affects churn behavior uniformly across all users, so the relative ranking of users by churn risk may still be valid, preserving AUC as a rank-based metric.
If the model will primarily be used during future promotional windows, evaluating on such a window is arguably appropriate.
Days since last login and viewing activity are genuine behavioral signals not driven solely by the promotion, providing real discriminative power.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: AUC drops by more than 0.05 when the model is evaluated on non-promotional months using the same feature set.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: AUC on non-promotional months is within 0.03 of the November–December estimate, indicating the promotional window is not materially distorting ranking performance.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: AUC drops slightly (0.03–0.05) but the model still substantially outperforms the rule-based baseline on non-promotional months.

must_not_claim: The model suffers from target leakage via the promotional feature
Family sub-account linkage is a problematic proxy for churn
The gradient boosting algorithm is inappropriate for this task

ground_truth_rationale: The core problem is a temporal/seasonal confound: the evaluation window systematically differs from the deployment distribution because a known external event (promotional campaign) suppresses churn during that specific period. This is a straightforward temporal distribution shift, not a modeling error, so the critique is correct. The defense point about AUC being rank-based has merit and makes the resolution empirical rather than purely logical.
notes: Easy case; temporal confound is explicitly surfaced in the prompt via the reviewer's observation. Tests whether the model can articulate why a seasonally unrepresentative holdout is a confound rather than just a test-set-size problem. Red herring: family sub-account feature looks suspicious as a proxy but is a valid behavioral signal.

task_prompt: An online payments company built a logistic regression model to detect fraudulent transactions. The training set contains 2 million transactions from the first half of the year; the test set contains 500,000 transactions from the second half. Fraud prevalence is 0.8% in training and 0.7% in test. The model achieves 0.93 AUPRC on the test set. Feature engineering included the transaction amount, merchant category code (MCC), device fingerprint hash, time-of-day bucket (morning/afternoon/evening/night), and a binary flag indicating whether the cardholder's billing country matches the merchant's country. The feature pipeline was reviewed by the compliance team prior to model deployment; no regulatory objections were raised. The model went live without a formal monitoring dashboard, with only weekly batch reports scheduled to review performance metrics. During a post-deployment review three months later, the team notices that AUPRC on live traffic is only 0.71. Investigation reveals that a new fraud ring began operating in month seven of the year, introducing a novel attack pattern not present in training data. The model's developers argue that the model was correctly evaluated and that the performance drop is entirely attributable to concept drift caused by the new fraud ring, which could not have been anticipated. A risk analyst counters that the evaluation methodology should have included adversarial robustness checks and an out-of-time validation on a recent holdout, and that the lack of a real-time monitoring system delayed detection of the degradation by nearly three months.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Both parties are partially correct: the evaluation did not include an out-of-time or adversarial holdout, which is a real methodological gap, but the sudden emergence of a novel fraud ring is a genuine distribution shift that no static evaluation could fully anticipate.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare model AUPRC on transactions from months 9–12 (post-ring) versus months 7–8 (pre-ring-emergence but post-training) to separate drift from baseline generalization quality.

planted_issues.description: The evaluation used a random or stratified split rather than a strict temporal holdout, so the test set does not reflect the sequential deployment scenario where training always precedes scoring.
No production monitoring or model refresh cadence was established, so the team discovered the performance degradation retrospectively rather than proactively.

ideal_critique: A fraud detection model should always be evaluated with a strict out-of-time split that mirrors the deployment scenario—training on earlier data and testing on strictly later data—because fraud patterns evolve and random splits leak future information about fraud cluster patterns into training.
The absence of a monitoring pipeline means the model degraded silently for three months, which in a fraud context represents real financial loss that a proper alerting system would have reduced.
Even without the new fraud ring, the evaluation as described does not validate the model's temporal generalization.
ideal_defense: The new fraud ring represents a genuine out-of-distribution event that no evaluation procedure conducted on historical data could have flagged in advance; this is a fundamental limitation of any supervised fraud model.
The 0.93 AUPRC on test data suggests the model generalizes well to the known fraud distribution, and the live degradation is attributable to a novel attack pattern rather than overfitting.
