The billing-country mismatch and device fingerprint features are robust signals for known fraud types, and the model's pre-ring performance in production (months 7–8) should be checked before concluding the evaluation was flawed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: AUPRC on months 7–8 (before the new fraud ring) is meaningfully lower than 0.93, indicating the model's offline evaluation was optimistic even without the new ring.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: AUPRC on months 7–8 is close to 0.93, confirming that the sharp drop coincides precisely with the new fraud ring's emergence and not with a pre-existing generalization gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: AUPRC on months 7–8 is moderately lower (e.g., 0.82–0.88), suggesting both baseline generalization issues and concept drift are present simultaneously.

must_not_claim: The class imbalance (0.8% fraud) invalidates AUPRC as a metric for this task
Logistic regression is too simple for fraud detection and must be replaced
The billing-country mismatch feature is a discriminatory proxy variable

ground_truth_rationale: The case is genuinely mixed because both the evaluation gap (no out-of-time split) and the unforeseen concept drift are real and separable. The correct position is not purely critique because the novel fraud ring represents a legitimate argument for the defense. The planted issues are real methodological weaknesses that a proper review should surface, but they do not fully explain the production drop.
notes: Easy case with a mixed verdict. Red herring: the billing-country mismatch feature appears to be a potential discriminatory proxy, but it is a standard, legally used fraud signal in card-not-present transactions. Tests ability to separate drift from evaluation methodology failures.

task_prompt: A fintech company ran a 4-week A/B test to evaluate a new ML-powered loan offer personalization feature. Users were randomly assigned to either the control group (static offer displayed to all eligible users) or the treatment group (ML model selects the offer amount and interest rate shown). The primary metric is click-through rate (CTR) on the offer banner. Treatment group CTR was 14.2% versus control CTR of 11.0%, yielding a relative lift of 29% (p < 0.001, two-sided z-test for proportions). The ML model used to personalize offers was trained on 18 months of historical loan application data. The experiment ran from November 1 to November 28. The randomization was performed at the user level using a hash-based assignment to ensure each user consistently received the same experience across sessions. Traffic to the experiment totaled 84,000 unique users across both arms. The data science team recommends full rollout, citing the large and statistically significant lift. A senior analyst observes that November contains two major shopping holidays (Black Friday falls on November 27 in this instance) and that users browsing the platform during holiday shopping periods may have unusually high purchase intent, inflating CTR for any offer regardless of personalization quality. The team responds that randomization ensures both groups experience the same holiday traffic, so the experiment remains valid.

ground_truth.final_verdict: While randomization controls for between-group confounding, the experiment window's heavy overlap with Black Friday creates a novelty-and-intent confound that makes the measured lift unrepresentative of the model's steady-state performance.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-run the experiment for at least 4 weeks on a non-holiday period (e.g., January–February) and compare the treatment effect size to the November estimate.

planted_issues.description: The experiment ran almost entirely during the holiday shopping season, when user purchase intent is abnormally elevated; the measured lift may not generalize to typical months.
Users in the treatment group encountering personalized offers for the first time may respond more positively due to novelty rather than genuine personalization quality, inflating short-term CTR.
CTR on the offer banner does not measure whether users actually took the loan or benefited financially; optimizing CTR may select for offers that attract clicks but not conversions or repayment.

ideal_critique: Randomization controls for confounding between treatment and control groups within the experiment, but it does not address external validity: a lift measured entirely during a holiday period may not generalize to the remaining 11 months of the year.

The novelty effect is a well-documented phenomenon in A/B testing where users respond to change itself rather than to the substantive improvement; a 4-week test on a never-before-seen personalized interface does not allow the novelty effect to decay.
CTR is a noisy proxy for business value; a model that shows high-interest offers to high-intent holiday shoppers may inflate CTR without increasing approved loans or reducing defaults.
The team's response conflates internal validity (randomization achieves) with external validity (randomization does not achieve), which is the correct statistical distinction to surface.
ideal_defense: Within the experimental window, randomization guarantees that any difference in CTR between groups is causally attributable to the treatment, not to holiday traffic, because both groups face identical holiday conditions.
If the product will be deployed year-round including during future holiday seasons, measuring performance during holidays is partially relevant.
The statistical significance (p < 0.001) is robust and the sample size is large, making the within-window result reliable.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The treatment effect (relative CTR lift) is less than 15% in a replicated January–February experiment, suggesting the November lift was substantially holiday-inflated.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The treatment effect in January–February is within 5 percentage points of the November estimate, indicating the personalization model generalizes well across seasons.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The January–February lift is 15–24%, suggesting partial external validity but non-trivial seasonal amplification.

must_not_claim: Randomization was not properly implemented because of the holiday confound
The ML personalization model is overfit to historical data because it was trained on 18 months
The z-test for proportions is the wrong statistical test for this scenario

ground_truth_rationale: The team's defense conflates internal and external validity. Randomization is valid within the window, but the external validity critique is correct: a model evaluated only during peak-intent holidays cannot be confidently generalized to year-round deployment. The novelty effect and CTR-vs-conversion issues compound the concern. This is a temporal/seasonal confound on external validity rather than internal validity.
notes: Medium difficulty; requires knowing the internal vs. external validity distinction in A/B testing. Red herring: the 18-month training window looks potentially stale or potentially too long, but it is not the core issue. Temporal confound case #2 of 4.

task_prompt: Researchers at a regional hospital network trained a random forest model to predict 30-day readmission risk for heart failure patients. The dataset contains 12,000 discharge episodes from three hospitals over five years. Features include patient age, gender, ejection fraction, number of prior admissions in the past year, length of stay, discharge disposition (home, skilled nursing facility, rehabilitation), number of medications at discharge, serum creatinine level, BNP level, and the attending physician's specialty (cardiologist vs. hospitalist vs. internist). All three hospitals participate in the same EHR system, enabling consistent feature extraction across sites. The model achieves 0.79 AUC on a held-out random 20% test split. The authors report that physician specialty is one of the top five features by Gini importance and interpret this as evidence that cardiologist-managed patients have better post-discharge coordination, recommending that the hospital network expand cardiologist coverage for heart failure patients. However, Hospital A is a tertiary referral center that predominantly employs cardiologists and takes on the most complex patients transferred from other facilities, while Hospitals B and C are community hospitals staffed primarily by hospitalists and internists treating lower-acuity cases. The patient complexity at Hospital A, as measured by ejection fraction quartile and prior admission count, is substantially higher than at the community hospitals. Discharge disposition is included as a feature, and the majority of Hospital A patients are discharged to skilled nursing or rehabilitation facilities, while most Hospital B and C patients are discharged home. A reviewer asks whether physician specialty is an independent predictor or a proxy for hospital site and case complexity.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Physician specialty's predictive importance is largely confounded by case complexity and hospital-level selection effects, making the causal interpretation about cardiologist coordination unwarranted, though the model's predictive performance may still be valid for risk stratification.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Refit the model stratified by hospital site and acuity tier (e.g., ejection fraction quartile, number of prior admissions), and examine whether physician specialty retains predictive importance within strata.

planted_issues.description: Physician specialty is confounded with hospital site (tertiary vs. community) and patient acuity, so its high Gini importance reflects case-mix differences rather than causal effects of specialty care.

A random 20% split does not guarantee that test patients are from hospitals or time periods not represented in training, potentially allowing the model to learn hospital-specific fixed effects that inflate AUC.

Random forest Gini importance is known to be biased toward high-cardinality features and does not support causal claims; permutation importance or SHAP values with careful confound adjustment are needed for causal interpretation.

ideal_critique: Hospital A's use of cardiologists and its status as a tertiary referral center with complex patients are thoroughly conflated; physician specialty in this dataset is a proxy for hospital identity and case acuity, not an independent causal lever.

The random split evaluation leaks hospital-specific patterns into both training and test, meaning the 0.79 AUC likely overestimates how well the model would perform when deployed prospectively at a new hospital.

Gini importance in random forests does not have a causal interpretation and is biased toward features with many unique values; the recommendation to expand cardiologist coverage far exceeds what this analysis supports.
ideal_defense: Even if physician specialty is confounded, the model's risk scores may still be clinically useful for within-hospital ranking of patients by readmission risk, which does not require causal validity of individual features.

The dataset includes strong clinical features (ejection fraction, BNP, creatinine, prior admissions) that capture genuine disease severity, supporting the predictive AUC regardless of the specialty confound.
The authors could adjust for hospital site as a fixed effect and recheck specialty's importance, which would be a straightforward robustness check rather than invalidating the entire study.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Physician specialty importance drops to near zero after stratifying by hospital site or including hospital as a covariate, confirming it was acting as a hospital proxy.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Physician specialty retains meaningful importance within each hospital stratum (i.e., among patients at Hospital A, cardiologist management still predicts lower readmission), indicating a residual signal beyond site confounding.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Specialty importance is reduced but not eliminated after site adjustment, leaving uncertainty about how much of the signal is genuine versus confounded.

must_not_claim: BNP and creatinine levels should be excluded because they are post-admission measurements that constitute target leakage
The dataset is too small (12,000 episodes) to train a random forest model reliably
Gender should be removed as a feature due to fairness concerns

ground_truth_rationale: The predictive model may be valid for risk stratification, but the causal interpretation of physician specialty is not supported because of the hospital-level selection confound. This is a classic example of ecological confounding embedded in a multi-site observational study. The correct position is mixed because the critique of the causal claim is warranted while the defense of the model's predictive utility has merit.
notes: Medium difficulty. Red herring: BNP and creatinine look like potential target leakage (measured close to discharge) but both are standard discharge-time clinical values included appropriately in readmission prediction. Multi-acceptable-resolutions case.

task_prompt: A music streaming platform replaced its collaborative filtering recommendation engine with a deep learning model that incorporates both user-item interaction history and audio content features (tempo, key, energy, danceability derived from a pretrained audio encoder). The platform's catalog contains approximately 80 million tracks, and the prior collaborative filtering system had been in production for three years. The new model was evaluated using an offline retrospective protocol: the most recent 10% of each user's interaction history was held out as a test set, and the model was trained on the preceding 90%. The team reports an improvement in Recall@10 from 0.23 (collaborative filtering baseline) to 0.31, evaluated over a random sample of 200,000 active users. The content team is pleased and notes that the audio features should allow the model to recommend newly released songs that lack interaction history, solving the cold-start problem. A research scientist reviewing the setup notes that the model's training objective was to predict the next song a user would play, and that the platform's existing recommendation system was responsible for surfacing most of the training interactions. She raises concerns about exposure bias in the historical interaction data, pointing out that songs never surfaced by the prior system are absent from training regardless of latent user preference, and she questions whether the offline metric improvement will translate to online engagement improvements.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The offline Recall@10 improvement is real but likely overstated due to exposure bias in historical interaction data, and the cold-start claim requires separate validation on truly new items; the model still merits an online A/B test.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run an online A/B test measuring stream completion rate and 30-day retention as primary metrics, with a dedicated cold-start sub-analysis on songs released after the training cutoff.

planted_issues.description: Users can only interact with songs the previous recommender surfaced; songs never recommended are absent from training data regardless of user preference, biasing the model toward the prior system's item distribution.

Offline Recall@10 measures how well the model predicts the historically exposed items a user chose, not how users would respond to genuinely novel recommendations, making online lift likely lower than offline improvement.
The claim that audio content features solve cold-start is asserted but not tested; the model was evaluated only on items with historical interaction data in the retrospective split.

ideal_critique: Exposure bias means the training data reflects what the old recommender showed users, not user preferences over the full catalog; the new model learns to approximate the old system's selections, and offline evaluation on these same interactions will not reveal whether it surfaces genuinely better items.

The cold-start claim is not supported by the evaluation protocol because all test items in the retrospective split are items that users had already interacted with, meaning they had interaction history during training.
Recall@10 is not a business metric; users playing a recommended song to completion or returning to the platform the following week are more meaningful signals of recommendation quality.
ideal_defense: Audio content features are genuinely complementary to collaborative filtering, especially for long-tail items and new releases, and the integration is a sound architectural choice.
The Recall@10 improvement of 34% relative to the baseline is large enough to be practically meaningful even if partially inflated by exposure bias.
The model still recommends more diverse items by incorporating content features, which may reduce popularity bias even if offline metrics understate this benefit.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Online A/B test shows less than 5% relative improvement in stream completion rate, or cold-start analysis shows the model fails on items released after the training cutoff.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Online A/B test shows greater than 10% improvement in stream completion rate and cold-start performance (on post-cutoff releases) is meaningfully better than the baseline.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Online stream completion improves 5–10% but cold-start performance is not better than the collaborative filtering baseline, indicating the content features help existing users but not new items.

must_not_claim: The audio encoder features (tempo, energy, danceability) are target leakage from the outcome variable
Collaborative filtering is always superior to content-based models for music recommendation
The 90/10 temporal split is flawed because it should be a random split instead

ground_truth_rationale: The case is mixed because the exposure bias critique is valid but does not completely invalidate the model—it warrants an online test rather than rejection. The cold-start claim is a scope overreach given the evaluation protocol. The correct path forward is empirical.
notes: Medium difficulty. Tests knowledge of exposure/position bias in recommendation evaluation, a nuanced offline-online gap concept. No red herring features planted to keep case focused on the structural bias issue.

task_prompt: A pharmaceutical research group trained a gradient boosted model to predict which patients with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes would develop diabetic kidney disease (DKD) within five years. Training data came from a large electronic health record system covering 85,000 patients diagnosed between 2005 and 2015; test data covered 18,000 patients diagnosed between 2016 and 2018. Features include HbA1c at diagnosis, eGFR at diagnosis, systolic blood pressure, BMI, LDL cholesterol, ACE inhibitor or ARB use at time of diagnosis, statin use at time of diagnosis, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR), race/ethnicity, age, sex, and insurance type. All patients in the study were followed for a minimum of five years from diagnosis date, and DKD was defined as a sustained eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m² or UACR above 300 mg/g on two consecutive measurements at least 90 days apart. The model achieves 0.84 AUC on the test set, with well-calibrated predicted probabilities across risk deciles. The authors note that ACE inhibitor/ARB use is one of the top predictors and interpret this as the model learning that patients on nephroprotective medications have higher underlying kidney risk. They recommend using the model to identify candidates for intensified ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy. A nephrologist reviewer objects that the relationship between medication use at baseline and subsequent DKD is more complex than the authors acknowledge, and that the deployment recommendation conflates prediction with intervention.

ground_truth.final_verdict: ACE inhibitor/ARB use at diagnosis is a confounded predictor because it is both a marker of pre-existing nephropathy risk (indication bias) and a preventive treatment; the causal direction is ambiguous, and recommending the model to guide intensification therapy is an intervention claim the predictive model cannot support.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Stratify the model's predicted risk scores by baseline UACR and eGFR category, and conduct a propensity-score-matched analysis comparing 5-year DKD rates among ACE inhibitor/ARB users versus non-users within the same predicted risk stratum.

planted_issues.description: ACE inhibitor/ARB use at diagnosis is prescribed preferentially to patients already showing signs of renal involvement (elevated UACR, borderline eGFR), making it a marker of higher baseline kidney risk rather than a causal predictor of DKD; this is a classic indication bias (channeling bias) confound.

The authors recommend using the model's risk scores to identify candidates for intensified therapy, but a predictive model trained on observational data cannot determine whether intensifying ACE inhibitor/ARB treatment in high-risk patients will prevent DKD.

Race/ethnicity in this clinical context captures socioeconomic access to care, dietary patterns, and historical under-treatment that are genuine risk mediators, but including it without adjustment for these pathways can mask inequitable model behavior and obscures the true causal structure.

ideal_critique: Indication bias (channeling bias) means ACE inhibitor/ARB use predicts DKD partly because physicians prescribed it to patients they already suspected had elevated renal risk; UACR and eGFR at diagnosis likely mediate this relationship, and the model is partially double-counting the renal risk signal.

The drug's protective effect may be masked by the indication confound: patients on ACE inhibitors/ARBs may have worse unadjusted DKD rates than untreated patients but better rates than untreated patients of equal underlying risk—an omitted variable bias classic in pharmacoepidemiology.

Recommending the model to guide therapeutic intensification is a causal claim: it asserts that high model-score patients will benefit from more aggressive medication, which requires a randomized trial or at minimum a well-controlled quasi-experimental study, not a predictive AUC.
Race/ethnicity's inclusion without pathway decomposition risks embedding structural health inequities into risk scores that then justify differential care.
ideal_defense: The model's predictive AUC of 0.84 is clinically meaningful for stratifying patients by DKD risk, regardless of whether ACE inhibitor/ARB use is causally or confoundedly predictive.

Physicians prescribing ACE inhibitors at diagnosis based on early renal signals are using clinical judgment that is genuinely informative about risk, so the feature captures a real signal even if its mechanism is confounded.
The recommendation to intensify therapy could still be reasonable if interpreted as flagging high-risk patients for closer nephrology follow-up rather than as a direct prescription directive.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After propensity-score matching on UACR and eGFR, ACE inhibitor/ARB users have lower or equal DKD rates compared to non-users, confirming the protective effect was masked by indication bias in the raw model interpretation.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Within matched strata, ACE inhibitor/ARB use remains positively associated with DKD even after adjusting for baseline renal markers, suggesting the feature captures residual risk beyond indication.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The association flips direction within some UACR/eGFR strata but not others, indicating heterogeneous indication bias that varies by baseline risk level.

must_not_claim: HbA1c at diagnosis is a form of target leakage because it measures disease severity at the same timepoint as the outcome
The model should not include sex as a feature because sex is a protected attribute
Insurance type should be excluded because it is not a clinical variable

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires domain knowledge of pharmacoepidemiology's indication bias (channeling bias), which is a specific and non-obvious confound pattern distinct from standard ML data leakage. The prediction-vs-intervention conflation is also present, making this a hard case with two distinct technical issues. Race/ethnicity as a mediator proxy adds a third layer of methodological complexity.
notes: Hard case requiring clinical epidemiology knowledge (indication/channeling bias). Red herring: insurance type as a feature looks problematic as a non-clinical proxy but is a legitimate socioeconomic confounder used in clinical risk modeling. Multi-acceptable-resolutions case.

task_prompt: A credit card issuer developed a neural network model to predict which cardholders will become profitable (defined as net interest income minus credit losses exceeding $200 per year) over a 12-month forward horizon. Training data spans 6 years and covers 400,000 cardholder-year observations. Features include credit score at account open, current credit utilization, payment behavior (always pays minimum, pays in full, revolves balance), number of transactions per month, average transaction size, account tenure, product type (cash-back, travel, co-branded retail), and whether the cardholder has any other products with the issuer (cross-sell flag). The model is a three-layer feedforward network with batch normalization and dropout, trained with early stopping on a 10% validation set. The model achieves 0.82 AUC on a held-out 15% random split. The business team plans to use the model scores to optimize credit line increases: cardholders with high predicted profitability scores will receive automatic credit line increases of 20–40% of their current line, with the goal of increasing revolving balances and interest income. The existing policy requires manual review for any line increase in the first 12 months of account tenure. A credit risk officer notes that the training data was collected under this conservative credit-line management policy and argues that deploying the model to drive an automated liberal line-increase policy will change the very behaviors (utilization, payment rate, default propensity) the model was trained to predict.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model was trained on behavior observed under a conservative credit-line policy; an automated liberal-increase deployment will alter payment behavior, utilization, and profitability in ways the model cannot predict, constituting a form of Goodhart's Law where optimizing on the model's scores changes the metric the model measures.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a controlled pilot where a randomly selected subset of high-score cardholders receives the automated line increase and track their 12-month profitability versus matched high-score cardholders who do not receive the increase.

planted_issues.description: Training data reflects behavior under a conservative credit-line policy; deploying the model to trigger aggressive line increases will change the distribution of utilization and payment behavior, invalidating the feature-to-profitability relationships learned during training.

Optimizing credit line increases toward high predicted-profitability scores will cause cardholders to increase their revolving balances, potentially increasing both interest income and credit losses in ways not captured by the historical model.

The cross-sell flag (whether the cardholder holds other products) is determined by the issuer's own past marketing decisions, not purely by cardholder characteristics; using it as a predictive feature introduces endogeneity because the issuer can manipulate this feature through its own actions.

ideal_critique: The training data is generated under a specific credit-line management policy; cardholders who never received a line increase may have behaved very differently (higher utilization, different payment patterns, increased default risk) had they received one, but this counterfactual behavior is unobserved.

This is a classic instance of Goodhart's Law applied to credit: when the model score becomes a target for credit line decisions, the behaviors that made the score predictive of profitability will change, degrading the model's calibration.

The cross-sell flag is endogenous because the issuer decides who to cross-sell; a cardholder with the flag may be more profitable partly because the issuer's own selection process identified them as a good candidate, not because cross-holding independently causes profitability.
ideal_defense: A randomized pilot (as recommended by the credit risk officer) would allow the team to directly measure the treatment effect of the credit line increase on profitability, sidestepping the endogeneity concern.
The model's feature set captures genuine financial behavior (credit score, payment patterns, utilization) that should remain predictive of profitability even under a changed line-increase policy.
The AUC of 0.82 indicates strong discriminative power; the concern is about calibration under policy shift, not about rank-ordering ability.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The randomized pilot shows that high-score cardholders who received line increases have lower 12-month profitability than the model predicted (or than matched controls who did not receive increases), due to higher credit losses.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: High-score cardholders with line increases achieve profitability close to model predictions and significantly higher than the control group, indicating the behavioral predictions are robust to the policy change.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Line-increase recipients show higher gross interest income but also higher credit losses, resulting in flat net profitability that matches neither a clear win nor a clear failure prediction.

must_not_claim: The 15% random split evaluation is invalid because it should use a temporal split
Neural networks are inappropriate for tabular credit data because tree-based models are universally superior
Credit score at account open constitutes target leakage because it predicts credit outcomes

ground_truth_rationale: The core issue is a policy feedback confound: the model's predictions are only valid under the policy regime in which training data was collected. Deploying the model to change the policy creates a distribution shift that the model cannot anticipate. This requires understanding Goodhart's Law, policy-induced distribution shift, and credit endogeneity—domain-specific knowledge beyond standard ML methodology.
notes: Hard case requiring credit risk domain knowledge (policy feedback loops, Goodhart's Law in credit, endogeneity of issuer-controlled features). No red herring features needed; the case complexity is sufficient.

task_prompt: An energy trading firm built an LSTM-based model to forecast day-ahead electricity spot prices for a European power market. The model was trained on hourly spot price data from January 2015 through December 2021 and validated on 2022 data. Features include lagged spot prices (1h, 24h, 168h), day-of-week and hour-of-day indicators, weather variables (temperature, wind speed, solar irradiance from regional stations), natural gas futures prices, and coal futures prices. On the 2022 validation set the model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 8.3%, compared to a naive 24h-lag baseline MAPE of 14.1%. The team deploys the model in January 2023 and observes that MAPE rises to 31% in the first quarter of 2023. Post-hoc analysis shows that during 2022 European natural gas prices increased by over 400% following geopolitical disruptions, fundamentally altering the price formation mechanism: gas peaker plants that previously set marginal prices were replaced by demand response and emergency imports at structurally different price levels. The team argues that the model performed well on 2022 validation data and that the 2023 regime change is an unforeseeable black-swan event. A quantitative researcher disagrees, noting that the 2022 validation set itself was already mid-crisis and that the model was evaluated on data that partially overlapped with the regime shift.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model was evaluated on 2022 data that was already structurally different from the 2015–2021 training regime; strong 2022 validation MAPE reflects the model partially adapting to early crisis dynamics, not robustness, and the 2023 performance collapse was at least partially foreseeable from the validation methodology's failure to test out-of-regime generalization.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate model performance separately on pre-crisis 2022 data (January–February 2022, before the major price spike) versus mid-crisis 2022 data (March–December 2022) to determine whether the 2022 MAPE hides structural instability.

planted_issues.description: The 2022 validation set was collected during an ongoing energy crisis when the price formation mechanism was already shifting; the model may have been implicitly trained to handle the early regime shift, making the validation MAPE optimistic for true out-of-regime performance.

Natural gas futures prices as a feature assume that the historical relationship between gas prices and spot electricity prices (gas sets marginal cost) holds in the deployment period, but when gas peakers stop being marginal setters this relationship breaks down entirely.
MAPE is distorted when actual prices are near zero or extremely elevated; during volatile crisis periods, MAPE can misrepresent forecast accuracy compared to MAE or RMSE.

ideal_critique: The validation set is contaminated by the very regime shift that caused the 2023 failure; a model that happens to learn the early-2022 price spike pattern from a short validation window is not demonstrating robust generalization—it is fitting to an in-distribution version of the crisis.

The economic assumption embedded in the gas futures feature—that gas peakers set marginal prices—is not a stable physical law but a market structure assumption that breaks down under extreme conditions; this structural dependency was not stress-tested.

Framing 2023 as a black-swan event is partly motivated reasoning: geopolitical disruptions that caused the gas crisis were ongoing and partially visible in 2022, and a robust validation protocol would have included out-of-regime stress tests.
ideal_defense: No statistical model trained on pre-crisis data can be expected to generalize to a 4x structural shift in the underlying economic mechanism; this is a genuine out-of-distribution event beyond the model's advertised scope.

The 2022 validation MAPE of 8.3% is substantially better than the 24h-lag baseline even during a turbulent year, suggesting the model extracts real signal from weather and calendar features that is not regime-dependent.
Incorporating additional crisis-period features (e.g., LNG import volumes, carbon certificates) would be a natural model extension rather than evidence that the original evaluation was flawed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Pre-crisis 2022 MAPE (January–February) is significantly lower than post-onset MAPE (March–December 2022), indicating the model's apparent 2022 generalization was driven by early-crisis interpolation rather than structural robustness.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: MAPE is roughly uniform across early and late 2022, suggesting the model performed consistently across the full year and the 2023 collapse was truly discontinuous.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Pre-crisis 2022 MAPE is lower but the difference is modest (less than 3 percentage points), leaving it unclear whether the validation result was meaningfully biased.

must_not_claim: LSTM models are fundamentally unsuitable for electricity price forecasting
The 168-hour lag feature introduces look-ahead bias into the model
Natural gas futures should be excluded because they are forward-looking instruments

ground_truth_rationale: This is a temporal confound case where the validation set was itself within the confounding event (energy crisis), making the evaluation non-representative of stable-regime generalization. The correct position is mixed because the team has a legitimate point about regime change, but the validation methodology had an identifiable flaw. Domain knowledge of electricity market price formation mechanisms is required for the gas feature analysis.
notes: Hard case. Temporal confound case #4 of 4. Red herring: the 168-hour lag feature looks potentially problematic (weekly seasonality feature that could encode future information) but is a standard and valid feature in electricity forecasting. Requires energy market domain knowledge.

task_prompt: A social media platform trained a BERT-based toxicity classifier to detect toxic comments in English. The model was fine-tuned on 500,000 manually labeled comments drawn from the platform's own historical moderation queue; labels were assigned by a trained content review team following the platform's community standards policy. Each comment was reviewed by at least two independent raters, with disagreements resolved by a senior reviewer; inter-rater agreement was 88% on the binary toxic/non-toxic label. Test accuracy is 92% and AUROC is 0.96 on a held-out 10% split of the same labeled English-language dataset. The model's inference latency is under 20 milliseconds per comment, making it suitable for near-real-time comment screening. The content policy team announces the model will be deployed globally across all 47 language markets the platform operates in, with the English-trained model applied directly to comments in all languages, citing the 92% accuracy figure as justification for broad deployment. A machine learning engineer objects that the model was trained and evaluated exclusively on English-language content and that deploying it to score non-English text is not supported by the evaluation. The team responds that English is the dominant language on the platform, comprising roughly 60% of content volume, and that the accuracy number is high enough to justify broad deployment without additional language-specific evaluation. The engineer notes that the remaining 40% of content represents hundreds of millions of posts per day.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The 92% accuracy was measured only on English content; the claim that this supports global deployment across 47 language markets is a scope overreach—the evaluation provides no evidence about model behavior on non-English languages.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate the model's precision and recall on representative samples of non-English content from at least the top 10 languages by volume before any non-English deployment.

planted_issues.description: The model was evaluated exclusively on English text but is claimed to support global multilingual deployment, which is a direct scope overreach unsupported by any non-English evaluation.

ideal_critique: BERT's tokenizer and contextual representations are language-specific; a model fine-tuned on English moderation data will either produce near-random scores or systematically mis-classify non-English text as non-toxic simply because it cannot parse the tokens meaningfully.
The 92% accuracy figure is entirely internal to the English-language evaluation set; it provides zero information about precision or recall on any of the 46 other languages.
The team's argument that English is 60% of content volume means 40% of content—potentially hundreds of millions of posts—will be scored by a model with unknown and unmeasured performance.
ideal_defense: BERT was pretrained on multilingual data in many variants (e.g., mBERT); if the base model is multilingual, some cross-lingual transfer may occur even without fine-tuning on other languages.
The platform can monitor model outputs on non-English content post-deployment and pull back if error rates are unacceptable, treating initial deployment as a staged rollout.
Content in multiple languages may still be accurately classified on English-borrowed slang or mixed-language toxic patterns that BERT might recognize.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Model precision or recall on the top 5 non-English languages is below 70%, confirming the evaluation gap is a real performance gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Model precision and recall on non-English languages (especially those with significant English loanword content) are above 85%, indicating cross-lingual transfer is sufficient for deployment.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance varies widely by language (e.g., strong on Spanish/French due to loanwords, near-random on Thai/Arabic), making a blanket deployment decision inappropriate.

must_not_claim: The 500,000 training examples are insufficient to fine-tune BERT
AUROC of 0.96 indicates the model is overfit to the test split
Manual labeling introduces unacceptable subjectivity into the training data

ground_truth_rationale: This is a straightforward scope overreach: the evaluation was performed on English data and the deployment claim covers all languages. No methodological complexity is needed; the evaluation simply does not cover the deployment scope.
notes: Easy case. The scope mismatch is explicit (English evaluation, global deployment). No red herring features; simplicity is intentional for easy calibration.

task_prompt: A healthcare company developed a predictive model that assigns a 12-month hospitalization risk score to patients with type 2 diabetes based on medical claims data (medications dispensed, diagnoses recorded, healthcare utilization patterns). They ran a randomized controlled trial in which high-risk patients identified by the model (top quintile of predicted scores) were randomly assigned to either a care management intervention (treatment) or standard care (control). Patients in the treatment arm received proactive outreach from a care coordinator within 72 hours of enrollment and ongoing access to a nurse hotline. The trial enrolled 4,200 patients over 12 months and was powered to detect a 10% reduction in hospitalizations. The RCT results showed a statistically significant 17% reduction in the number of hospitalizations per patient-year in the treatment arm versus control (p < 0.01, incidence rate ratio from a negative binomial regression). The company's marketing materials and sales presentations state: 'Our AI model reduces hospitalizations by 17%.' A health economist reviewing the materials argues that the 17% reduction is attributable to the care management intervention program, not to the AI model per se, since the model merely identified who to target. She adds that if a simple clinical rule could identify an equally high-risk group, the same reduction might be achievable without the AI, and asks for evidence that the model's targeting is more efficient than simpler alternatives.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The 17% hospitalization reduction is caused by the care management intervention, not by the AI model; the model's contribution is targeting efficiency, and the claim 'our AI model reduces hospitalizations' conflates the model's predictive function with the intervention's causal effect.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare hospitalization rates between the top-quintile RCT population and a matched control population that received the intervention based on a simple clinical rule (e.g., prior hospitalizations > 1), to isolate the incremental value of the AI model's targeting over simpler alternatives.

planted_issues.description: The marketing claim attributes the RCT intervention effect directly to the AI model, conflating the model's role (identifying targets) with the care management program's causal effect on hospitalizations.

The RCT does not test whether the AI model's targeting is more efficient than simpler rules; a high-risk group identified by prior hospitalization count alone might yield a comparable or superior reduction at lower cost.

The 17% average reduction may mask heterogeneous treatment effects; patients at different risk score levels may respond differently to the intervention, and the model's calibration within the top quintile affects how well treatment benefits are distributed.

ideal_critique: The 17% reduction is a property of the care management intervention when applied to the AI-identified high-risk group; the AI model is a targeting mechanism, not a treatment—attributing the reduction to the model misrepresents the causal structure.

To claim the AI model specifically adds value, one would need a comparison arm where the same intervention is delivered to a high-risk group identified by a simple rule (or randomly); without this, the incremental contribution of the AI targeting is unmeasured.
This is a prediction-to-intervention conflation: the model predicts who is at risk; it does not cause or reduce the risk itself.
ideal_defense: The AI model's targeting is what makes the intervention scalable and cost-effective; without the model, the company cannot efficiently identify the high-risk population, so the model is instrumentally responsible for the reduction being achievable.
The marketing claim, while imprecise, communicates the practical outcome (hospitalizations reduced when using the system) which is what customers and health plans care about.
If the model had poor targeting (e.g., identified mostly low-risk patients), the 17% reduction would not have been achieved; thus model quality is a necessary condition for the result.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A parallel arm using a simple prior-hospitalization rule to identify high-risk patients and applying the same intervention achieves a similar or larger hospitalization reduction, showing the AI model's targeting provides no incremental benefit.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The AI-targeted arm achieves a significantly larger hospitalization reduction than the simple-rule arm (e.g., >25% vs. 17%), demonstrating that AI targeting efficiency directly amplifies intervention impact.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The AI-targeted and simple-rule-targeted arms achieve similar reductions (within 3 percentage points), but the AI model identifies a different patient composition, making a purely quantitative comparison insufficient.

must_not_claim: The RCT is invalid because it was not double-blinded
Claims data is insufficient for hospitalization prediction and EHR data is required
The top quintile threshold for intervention targeting is arbitrary and should be optimized

ground_truth_rationale: This is a prediction-vs-intervention conflation case: the 17% reduction belongs to the care management program, and the model's value is in targeting efficiency which was not separately validated. The RCT design is sound; the scope of the claim made about the model's causal role is not.
notes: Medium difficulty. Prediction-vs-intervention conflation case #1 of 2. Tests understanding of the distinction between a model's targeting role and an intervention's causal effect in an RCT context.

task_prompt: A telecommunications company built a gradient boosted classification model to identify customers likely to churn within the next 60 days. The model was trained on 18 months of data covering 500,000 customers and uses features including contract type (monthly vs. annual), tenure in months, number of service calls in the past 90 days, monthly data usage, payment history (on-time, late, or missed payments in the past year), device age in months, and whether the customer has a bundled service (TV, internet, and mobile together). The model was tuned using 5-fold cross-validation and evaluated on a temporal holdout covering the final 3 months of the training period. On this holdout the model achieves 0.85 AUC, precision of 0.62, and recall of 0.71 at a probability threshold of 0.45. Customers flagged by the model receive a proactive retention offer consisting of a 15% bill discount for three months, delivered via text message and in-app notification within 48 hours of scoring. After six months of deployment, the retention team analyzes results and reports that 58% of customers who were flagged by the model and received the discount renewed their contracts for at least 60 additional days. The team concludes: 'Our AI-driven retention program has a 58% success rate, proving the model accurately identifies churners and that the discount is effective.' A data scientist challenges this interpretation, noting that without a holdout group of flagged customers who did not receive the discount, the team cannot separate model targeting accuracy from the discount's persuasive effect.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The 58% renewal rate is a combined effect of model targeting and discount persuasion; without a no-treatment control among flagged customers, neither the model's targeting accuracy nor the discount's causal effect can be isolated, making the 'proves the model' claim a scope overreach while the deployment itself may still be commercially beneficial.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a randomized holdback experiment: among customers flagged by the model as high-churn-risk, randomly assign 20% to receive no discount and compare their renewal rates to the 80% who receive the discount, to isolate the causal effect of the discount.

planted_issues.description: All flagged customers received the discount; without a randomized no-treatment holdback among flagged customers, the 58% renewal rate cannot be attributed separately to model accuracy (correctly identifying churners) versus discount effectiveness (preventing churn regardless of targeting).

At 0.62 precision, approximately 38% of customers who received discounts were not actually going to churn; the company is spending retention budget on loyal customers who did not need incentivizing, and these customers' renewals inflate the 'success rate'.

The claim that 58% success proves the model 'accurately identifies churners' conflates two separate questions: whether the model correctly identifies at-risk customers (a precision/recall question) and whether the discount causes renewal (a causal question).

ideal_critique: Without a randomized holdback group, the 58% renewal rate reflects the combination of model targeting quality and discount effectiveness; some fraction of these renewals would have occurred without any offer, and some high-probability churners may have renewed anyway.

At 38% false positive rate (precision 0.62), the company is spending retention budget on a substantial fraction of customers who were not planning to churn; the true cost-effectiveness of the program cannot be evaluated without knowing how many discounts were given to non-churners.
The deployment has reasonable commercial logic, but claiming it proves the model's accuracy confuses a business outcome with a model evaluation result.
ideal_defense: A 58% renewal rate among at-risk customers is commercially meaningful regardless of attribution; even if some false positives received discounts, the net retention improvement and revenue effect may justify the program.
The model's 0.85 AUC and 0.71 recall on the held-out test set are valid independent evidence of targeting accuracy; the deployment outcome supplements rather than supplants this evidence.

Withholding the discount from a randomly selected 20% of flagged customers (who may then churn) could create customer service and business risk; a retrospective matched analysis may be a lower-risk alternative.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A randomized holdback shows that flagged customers who did not receive the discount renewed at a rate of 45% or higher, indicating the discount has a modest causal effect and many flagged customers were not actually on the verge of churning.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The no-discount renewal rate among flagged customers is below 25%, confirming the model's high-risk flag corresponds to genuine churn intent and the discount is causally driving the 58% renewal rate.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The no-discount renewal rate is 25–44%, suggesting some model targeting value and some discount effect, but insufficient to cleanly attribute the 58% outcome to either.

must_not_claim: The 0.45 probability threshold is too low and inflates the false positive rate inappropriately
Gradient boosting is an inappropriate model for churn prediction
18 months of training data is insufficient for a robust churn model

ground_truth_rationale: The case is mixed because the deployment may be commercially sound even though the evaluative claim overreaches what the data shows. The 58% success rate cannot be attributed to model accuracy without a holdback group. The mixed verdict reflects that the defense has a valid business argument while the critique of the scope of the interpretation is correct.
notes: Medium difficulty. Red herring: the 0.45 threshold looks like it was chosen to maximize recall at the expense of precision, but this is a deliberate and defensible business choice for retention programs. Tests ability to identify the missing counterfactual (no-treatment holdback) as the core issue.

task_prompt: A digital health startup trained a deep learning model on retinal fundus photographs to predict cardiovascular risk. The model was developed using data from the UK Biobank, a large prospective cohort study: 68,000 retinal fundus images from participants linked to 10-year cardiovascular event outcomes (myocardial infarction, stroke, cardiovascular death). Images were acquired with standardized mydriatic fundus cameras at enrollment. The model is a convolutional neural network with a ResNet-50 backbone fine-tuned on the Biobank data. The model achieves AUC of 0.74 for 10-year major adverse cardiovascular event (MACE) prediction, compared to 0.72 for the Framingham Risk Score computed on the same held-out test set (N = 13,600 participants). The startup's press release, distributed to medical journalists and sent to potential hospital system clients, states: 'Our AI can detect heart disease from eye scans.' A cardiologist reviewing the press release argues that predicting future cardiovascular event probability is not equivalent to detecting existing heart disease, and that the modest AUC improvement over a validated clinical score does not justify clinical deployment claims. The startup responds that the AUC difference is statistically significant (p = 0.03) and that the model opens a new non-invasive screening channel that does not require blood draws, physical examination, or a clinic visit, potentially expanding access to cardiovascular risk assessment.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model predicts 10-year cardiovascular risk, not the presence of heart disease; 'detect heart disease' is a misleading scope claim, and the AUC of 0.74 versus 0.72 for Framingham is a statistically significant but clinically marginal improvement that does not by itself support deployment.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate whether the model adds clinically meaningful net reclassification improvement (NRI) or integrated discrimination improvement (IDI) over the Framingham Risk Score in a prospective validation cohort, stratified by risk tier.

planted_issues.description: Predicting future cardiovascular event probability is not equivalent to detecting existing heart disease; the press release conflates a 10-year risk prediction task with a diagnostic claim.

A 0.02 AUC improvement over Framingham is statistically significant but clinically marginal; AUC does not directly translate to improved patient outcomes or meaningful reclassification of borderline-risk patients.
UK Biobank participants are a volunteer cohort with known healthy-volunteer bias; the model's performance may not generalize to broader clinical populations with higher comorbidity burden.

ideal_critique: Predicting a 10-year probability of a cardiovascular event is a risk stratification task; detecting heart disease implies identifying current pathology. These are clinically distinct tasks, and conflating them in a press release misleads patients and clinicians about what the tool actually does.

A 0.02 AUC difference (0.74 vs. 0.72) at p = 0.03 in a large dataset reflects statistical significance driven by sample size, not clinical meaningfulness; the relevant question is whether the model changes clinical decisions for any patients, which requires NRI or decision curve analysis.

The UK Biobank healthy-volunteer bias means that MACE rates in the study population are lower than in a clinical screening population, and the model's calibration may not transfer to higher-risk clinical settings.
ideal_defense: A non-invasive, scalable cardiovascular risk screening tool that requires only a retinal photograph could expand access to risk assessment in settings where blood draws and clinical workups are not feasible.

The AUC of 0.74 is a valid benchmark comparison on the same held-out dataset; the Framingham Risk Score requires age, sex, cholesterol, blood pressure, smoking status, and diabetes status, while the model needs only a photograph.
Statistical significance at a large sample is meaningful—it rules out chance as an explanation for the AUC difference.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: NRI on a prospective clinical cohort is not statistically significant, or the model's predicted risk scores correlate strongly with Framingham scores, adding no independent information beyond what a clinical risk score already captures.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: NRI is statistically significant and clinically meaningful (e.g., net reclassification > 5% among intermediate-risk patients), and the model captures retinal biomarkers that are independent of Framingham risk factors.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: NRI is significant in some risk strata but not others, suggesting the model adds value for specific subpopulations but not as a general replacement for clinical risk scoring.

must_not_claim: Deep learning models cannot achieve AUC above 0.70 on cardiovascular risk prediction from retinal images
The study should have used a 5-year rather than 10-year outcome horizon
Fundus photograph quality variability makes this task fundamentally infeasible

ground_truth_rationale: Two separate scope issues are present: the diagnostic vs. prognostic conflation in the press release, and the clinical significance of a marginal AUC improvement. Both are valid critique targets. The UK Biobank generalization concern adds a third layer. This is prediction-vs-intervention/diagnosis conflation case #2.
notes: Medium difficulty. Prediction-vs-diagnosis conflation (a variant of prediction-vs-intervention). Red herring: the non-invasive screening argument sounds compelling but does not address whether the model's predictions are clinically actionable.

task_prompt: An economics research team used a machine learning approach to estimate the causal effect of a government-funded job training program on participant earnings. They used a dataset of 15,000 program participants and 45,000 non-participants drawn from a national labor market survey administered annually. Both groups were observed over a 3-year follow-up period. To control for selection bias (workers who choose to participate may be systematically different from non-participants), the team trained a gradient boosted model to estimate propensity scores: the probability of participating in the program given observed covariates including age, years of education, pre-program annual earnings (three years of history), industry sector, region, and prior employment gaps. They matched participants to non-participants using 1:3 nearest-neighbor matching with a caliper of 0.05 standard deviations on the propensity score logit. The estimated average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) is a $4,200 increase in annual earnings, with a bootstrapped 95% confidence interval of [$3,100, $5,300]. After matching, all standardized mean differences for included covariates are below 0.1. The research team publishes the finding and concludes: 'The job training program causally increases participant earnings by approximately $4,200 per year.' A labor economist reviewer notes that the study's causal conclusions rest on the strong ignorability assumption and questions whether the survey's observed covariates fully capture the reasons workers select into the program, noting that individual motivation and geographic proximity to training centers are not measured.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The propensity score analysis is methodologically sound given observed covariates, but the causal claim requires the strong ignorability assumption (no unmeasured confounders); the study does not establish that this assumption holds, and plausible unmeasured confounders (motivation, geographic proximity to program, employer endorsement) could meaningfully bias the ATT estimate.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a formal sensitivity analysis (e.g., Rosenbaum bounds or E-value computation) to quantify how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain away the $4,200 ATT estimate; report the E-value alongside the estimate.

planted_issues.description: Propensity score matching identifies causal effects only under the strong ignorability assumption (all confounders are measured and included), which cannot be verified from observational data and is likely violated by unmeasured factors like individual motivation and access.

The team reports excellent covariate balance but does not conduct or report a sensitivity analysis quantifying robustness to unmeasured confounding, which is standard practice in propensity score studies.

The ATT estimated on matched pairs applies to the treated population; it cannot be generalized to all eligible workers or used to project program impacts for a different participant pool (e.g., if the program were scaled up).

ideal_critique: Propensity score matching controls for observed confounders but is entirely silent on unmeasured confounders; workers who self-select into training programs likely differ in motivation, career goals, and family support in ways not captured by the survey's covariates.

Covariate balance after matching means only that the matched groups look similar on observed variables; it provides no evidence that unobserved variables are balanced, and reporting only balance statistics without sensitivity analysis is a significant omission.

Scaling the conclusion to 'causally increases earnings' overstates what an observational propensity score study can establish; at best it demonstrates conditional association adjusting for observed confounders, and the word 'causally' requires either an instrument or a sensitivity analysis bounding the confounder impact.
ideal_defense: Propensity score matching with ML-estimated scores is a state-of-the-art method for observational causal inference; excellent post-matching covariate balance is the strongest available evidence of confound control given the data.
The $4,200 ATT estimate is consistent with randomized experimental estimates of similar training programs from prior literature, providing external corroboration.
The study covers a broad set of covariates including detailed employment history and regional controls, reducing the plausibility of large unmeasured confounders.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The E-value for the ATT estimate is below 2.0, meaning a relatively weak unmeasured confounder (risk ratio of 2.0) would be sufficient to explain away the estimated earnings effect.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The E-value exceeds 3.5, indicating that any unmeasured confounder would need to be implausibly strong (stronger than most observed confounders) to nullify the effect, supporting robustness of the causal claim.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The E-value is between 2.0 and 3.5, indicating moderate sensitivity to unmeasured confounding that neither clearly validates nor invalidates the causal claim.

must_not_claim: Gradient boosting is an inappropriate method for propensity score estimation because it can overfit
The 1:3 matching ratio inflates statistical power inappropriately
The caliper of 0.05 standard deviations is too strict and discards too many treated units

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires domain knowledge of causal inference methodology: the distinction between selection on observables (what propensity matching achieves) and strong ignorability (what causal claims require), and the role of sensitivity analysis tools like Rosenbaum bounds and E-values. The mixed verdict is appropriate because the methodology is correct given the data—the scope overreach is in the causal language, not in the matching procedure.
notes: Hard case requiring causal inference domain knowledge (strong ignorability, Rosenbaum bounds, E-values). Red herring: the 1:3 matching ratio and caliper value look potentially arbitrary but are standard choices with good methodological grounding. Multi-acceptable-resolutions case.

task_prompt: A mental health technology company fine-tuned a large language model (specifically a 7B-parameter instruction-tuned model) on a curated dataset of 120,000 de-identified therapy session transcripts from cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and motivational interviewing sessions to create an AI therapeutic assistant. The de-identification was performed using a validated NER-based pipeline; all patient identifiers were removed before fine-tuning. The model was evaluated on a held-out set of 5,000 session segments using two metrics: (1) Therapist Rating Score (TRS), where licensed therapists rated AI-generated responses on a 5-point Likert scale for empathy, appropriateness, and safety, yielding a mean TRS of 4.1/5.0; and (2) an automated metric comparing AI responses to gold-standard therapist responses using BERTScore (mean F1 = 0.81), computed using a domain-general encoder. The evaluation was conducted on isolated session segments rather than complete multi-session treatment courses. The company's deployment announcement states: 'Our AI therapeutic assistant performs at the level of a licensed therapist and is ready for deployment as a primary mental health support tool for anxiety and depression.' A clinical psychologist argues that TRS ratings on isolated segment responses do not capture therapeutic outcomes, the therapeutic alliance, or patient safety across a multi-session treatment course. An NLP researcher adds that BERTScore measures lexical and semantic similarity but does not validate clinical appropriateness, and that fine-tuning on transcripts does not test the model's real-world safety under adversarial or crisis conditions.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The evaluation measures response quality on isolated session segments and surface-level linguistic similarity, neither of which is sufficient to claim therapist-level performance or readiness for deployment as a primary mental health tool; the scope of the claim far exceeds what the evaluation protocol establishes.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a prospective randomized controlled trial measuring clinically validated outcomes (PHQ-9, GAD-7, therapeutic alliance scores) in real patients over a full treatment course (minimum 8 sessions) comparing the AI assistant to a waitlist control and to standard therapist care.

planted_issues.description: Evaluating response quality on isolated 5,000 session segments does not measure longitudinal therapeutic outcomes, treatment efficacy, patient-reported improvement, or safety over a full course of care.

BERTScore measures lexical/semantic similarity to reference responses; a therapeutically harmful response that paraphrases a good response may score highly, making BERTScore an invalid proxy for clinical safety or appropriateness.

Claiming the model performs 'at the level of a licensed therapist' requires a direct head-to-head comparison measuring patient outcomes, not a rating of isolated response segments by third-party raters.

A mental health AI deployed as a primary support tool must demonstrate safety with respect to suicide risk escalation, crisis response, and contraindicated interventions; none of these safety dimensions are addressed in the evaluation.

ideal_critique: A Likert-scale rating of isolated session segments by third-party therapists measures surface quality of individual responses, not whether a treatment course leads to symptom improvement, which is the actual definition of therapeutic performance.

BERTScore rewards responses that sound like therapist responses lexically and semantically; a model that produces plausible-sounding but clinically inappropriate responses (e.g., normalizing avoidance, missing suicidal ideation cues) can achieve high BERTScore while causing harm.

The parity claim requires an RCT with standardized outcome measures (PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) across a treatment course compared directly to licensed therapists; this study measures neither outcomes nor parity.

Deployment as a 'primary support tool' in mental health requires regulatory engagement (FDA in the US, UKCA in the UK), formal clinical validation, and explicit safety protocols for crisis scenarios—none of which are addressed.
ideal_defense: Expert clinician ratings of 4.1/5.0 on empathy, appropriateness, and safety represent a meaningful signal that the model generates contextually sensible therapeutic responses, which is a prerequisite for any clinical utility.
BERTScore combined with expert ratings provides multi-faceted evidence of response quality that goes beyond purely automated evaluation.
Fine-tuning on 120,000 real therapy transcripts grounds the model in genuine therapeutic language and interaction patterns rather than generic instruction-following.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a prospective trial, patients using the AI assistant as a primary tool do not show statistically significant improvement on PHQ-9/GAD-7 over a waitlist control, or the AI arm shows higher rates of adverse events (suicidal ideation escalation, dropout) than the therapist arm.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Patients using the AI assistant show PHQ-9 and GAD-7 improvements comparable to those receiving standard therapist care, and adverse event rates are not significantly elevated.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: PHQ-9/GAD-7 improvements are significant versus waitlist but significantly lower than therapist care, suggesting the AI has value as a supplementary but not primary tool.

must_not_claim: 7B-parameter models are too small for therapeutic applications and GPT-4 level models are required
De-identified therapy transcripts cannot be used for fine-tuning due to HIPAA
Instruction-tuned models are inherently unsuitable for mental health applications

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires clinical NLP domain knowledge: understanding that segment-level evaluation and BERTScore do not constitute clinical validation, that 'parity with therapists' requires outcome-based RCT evidence, and that mental health AI deployment has specific regulatory and safety requirements. Four separate high-severity planted issues make this a rich hard case.
notes: Hard case requiring domain knowledge at the intersection of clinical psychology and NLP evaluation. Red herring: de-identification and HIPAA issues are commonly raised for medical AI but are not the central methodological problem here. Four must-find items.

task_prompt: A credit risk team at a mid-size consumer lending company developed a gradient-boosted tree model to predict 90-day default on personal loans. The training set covers 36 months of loan originations (Jan 2020–Dec 2022), comprising 180,000 loans with a 6.8% default rate. The team used stratified 5-fold cross-validation and reported AUC-ROC of 0.847 with a 95% confidence interval of [0.841, 0.853]. Features include borrower income, debt-to-income ratio, credit bureau score, months-since-last-delinquency, and loan amount. During the review, a senior data scientist noted that the team split the data chronologically before cross-validation folds—that is, each fold's validation set came from a later time window than its training set, rather than random shuffling. The reviewer flagged this as 'non-standard cross-validation methodology' and suggested it inflated the difficulty of the task, causing the model to appear worse than it actually is. The team countered that they deliberately chose time-ordered folds to simulate real deployment conditions where predictions are made on future borrowers whose characteristics may shift. The documentation includes a pre-analysis protocol, written before model training began, that specifies: 'validation splits will respect temporal ordering to estimate generalization to future loan cohorts.' The team asks you to evaluate whether their cross-validation methodology is sound and whether the critic's concern is valid.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Chronological cross-validation fold ordering is methodologically superior for time-series financial data and explicitly pre-specified, making the critic's objection unfounded.

planted_issues.description: The critic flags chronological fold ordering as non-standard, but this is actually the correct approach for temporal data—random shuffling would cause data leakage by allowing future information to appear in training folds, making the concern entirely backwards.

ideal_critique: Random-shuffle cross-validation would constitute temporal leakage in loan default modeling because future borrower vintages can inform past fold training sets, inflating apparent performance.
The team's pre-specified protocol showing temporal ordering is the methodologically appropriate choice for production loan models.
The only legitimate disclosure is that the held-out performance may still not capture macro-economic regime shifts beyond the 36-month window.
ideal_defense: Chronological splits are standard best practice for financial time-series models per regulatory guidance (e.g., SR 11-7) and academic literature on credit risk.
Random shuffling would leak future label information into training, causing overly optimistic and unreliable performance estimates.
The pre-analysis protocol formally pre-committed to this choice, eliminating any post-hoc rationalization concern.
The resulting AUC of 0.847 is therefore a conservative, realistic estimate of deployment performance.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Random-shuffle CV were shown to produce identical or lower AUC, which would suggest temporal ordering has no practical effect (unlikely given domain).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Random-shuffle CV produces materially higher AUC than chronological CV, confirming that temporal ordering reduces leakage-inflated optimism.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Both CV approaches produce nearly identical AUC within 0.002, suggesting the specific loans in this dataset happen to be temporally stable.

must_not_claim: chronological_cv_is_methodological_error
temporal_fold_ordering_inflates_difficulty_invalidating_results
standard_kfold_shuffle_is_superior_for_loan_data

suspicious_but_valid_features: Time-ordered cross-validation folds look non-standard to reviewers unfamiliar with financial ML, but they are the correct approach for loan default modeling: random shuffling causes future-into-past leakage. The pre-specified protocol written before training began confirms this was a deliberate, justified design choice rather than post-hoc rationalization.
ground_truth_rationale: Chronological cross-validation is the methodologically required approach for temporal financial data to prevent leakage and to simulate deployment generalization. The pre-analysis protocol removes any ambiguity about whether this was a post-hoc rationalization. The critic's concern, while superficially plausible to those unfamiliar with time-series ML, is factually incorrect.
notes: Red herring: non-standard-looking CV is actually the right choice. Tests whether evaluator correctly understands temporal leakage in financial ML. Pre-specified analysis choice justification (rule 6).

task_prompt: A hospital system's data science team built a binary classifier to identify patients at high risk of 30-day readmission after discharge for congestive heart failure (CHF). The dataset includes 22,400 admissions from a single large urban academic medical center over four years. The team reports a model sensitivity of 0.82 and specificity of 0.74 at the chosen operating threshold. A peer reviewer observed that the team chose a threshold of 0.35 (rather than the conventional 0.50) and labeled this as 'threshold manipulation to inflate sensitivity metrics.' The team's documentation explains that before any model training, their clinical operations team determined via a cost-benefit analysis that a missed high-risk patient (false negative) costs approximately 4.2× more than an unnecessary care-coordination intervention for a low-risk patient (false positive), due to the high cost of readmission versus the modest cost of a phone-based care coordination call. Using the standard expected-cost formula for a binary classifier, the 0.35 threshold was mathematically derived from this 4.2:1 cost ratio prior to model development. The team's model development log timestamps this cost-matrix specification one month before any data was analyzed. You are asked to evaluate whether the threshold choice is methodologically justified or whether the reviewer's manipulation concern is valid.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The threshold of 0.35 is mathematically derived from a pre-specified asymmetric cost matrix, making the reviewer's manipulation claim unfounded.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer claims the non-standard 0.35 threshold is 'manipulation,' but this threshold was derived mathematically from a pre-specified 4.2:1 cost ratio reflecting real asymmetric clinical costs—this is precisely the correct use of decision-threshold optimization in medical ML.

ideal_critique: Reporting sensitivity and specificity at a non-default threshold without disclosing the AUC-ROC curve could mislead readers who assume a 0.50 threshold.
The cost ratio of 4.2:1 was estimated by the clinical operations team and may not reflect the true economic costs, but this is a disclosure item, not a methodological flaw.
The model is trained on a single academic medical center; applicability to community hospitals requires additional validation.
ideal_defense: The optimal threshold for a binary classifier under asymmetric costs is p* = cost_FN / (cost_FN + cost_FP), which with a 4.2:1 ratio yields approximately 0.81/(0.81+0.19) ≈ 0.81... simplified correctly to ~0.35 after normalization, which the team correctly computed.
The cost matrix was pre-specified before model development, eliminating retrospective threshold shopping.
The full ROC curve is available in the supplementary materials, so no information is concealed.
Reporting metrics at the operationally relevant threshold is standard in clinical decision support development.
