ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The cost-matrix timestamp post-dates any exploratory data analysis showing sensitivity results, which would suggest the threshold was chosen after observing metric values.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The cost-matrix specification timestamp predates all model training and data analysis by at least two weeks, confirming the pre-specification claim.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The cost matrix was written at the same time as exploratory analysis with no audit trail separating them.

must_not_claim: threshold_below_0.5_is_always_manipulation
non_default_threshold_invalidates_reported_metrics
asymmetric_cost_threshold_choice_requires_external_validation_before_use

suspicious_but_valid_features: A threshold of 0.35 looks like cherry-picking to reviewers expecting 0.50, but it is the mathematically correct operating point given the pre-specified 4.2:1 asymmetric cost matrix. The pre-specification timestamp confirms this was not post-hoc threshold shopping.
ground_truth_rationale: Asymmetric cost matrices justify non-standard probability thresholds in clinical ML; this is standard practice in health economics-informed model deployment. The pre-specification eliminates the post-hoc rationalization concern. The correct critique is about potential cost-estimate accuracy and single-site generalizability, neither of which invalidates the methodology.
notes: Pre-specified cost matrix justifies non-standard threshold (rule 6). Red herring: low threshold looks like metric gaming. Easy case: the justification is clearly documented.

task_prompt: A fintech startup built a fraud detection model for card-not-present (CNP) transactions using a random forest classifier trained on 14 months of transaction data (approximately 9.4 million transactions, 0.23% fraud rate). The dataset includes features such as transaction amount, merchant category code, time since last transaction, device fingerprint, and geographic distance from the most recent prior transaction. They evaluated the model using area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) rather than AUC-ROC, reporting an AUPRC of 0.61. A code reviewer flagged this choice, stating that 'AUPRC is a niche metric and the team should report the standard AUC-ROC to allow comparison with published literature, and that selecting AUPRC appears to be cherry-picking a metric where the model looks better.' The team's pre-analysis document states: 'Given a highly imbalanced class distribution (fraud prevalence ~0.23%), AUC-ROC can give misleadingly optimistic results because it accounts for true negatives, which are abundant; AUPRC is the appropriate primary metric for this regime as it focuses on precision and recall for the minority class.' The document was filed with their model governance committee six weeks before training began. Both metrics are reported in the model card: AUC-ROC = 0.963, AUPRC = 0.61. The divergence between the two metrics is large—AUC-ROC of 0.963 versus AUPRC of 0.61—which the reviewer also cited as suspicious. Evaluate whether the metric selection is appropriate, whether cherry-picking occurred, and whether the large divergence between metrics is itself evidence of a problem.

ground_truth.final_verdict: AUPRC is the methodologically correct primary metric for severely imbalanced fraud detection, and selecting it was pre-specified and appropriately disclosed alongside AUC-ROC.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer claims AUPRC was cherry-picked to make the model look better, but at 0.23% prevalence AUC-ROC is known to produce inflated optimistic estimates because the vast true-negative pool dominates; AUPRC is the standard recommendation for severe class imbalance, and the choice was pre-specified.

ideal_critique: The high AUC-ROC of 0.963 in the context of 0.23% fraud rate is exactly the optimism artifact AUPRC was chosen to avoid.
Reporting both metrics in the model card actually strengthens transparency rather than indicating cherry-picking.
The main legitimate concern is that absolute AUPRC values depend on prevalence and may not be directly comparable across datasets with different fraud rates.
ideal_defense: At 0.23% fraud prevalence, AUC-ROC is known to produce deceptively high scores even for mediocre models because true negatives dominate the ROC curve.
AUPRC is the widely recommended primary metric for imbalanced classification (Davis & Goadrich 2006; Saito & Rehmsmeier 2015).
Pre-specifying AUPRC before training eliminates the possibility of post-hoc metric selection.
Both metrics are disclosed, so no information is withheld from reviewers.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The pre-analysis document was actually created after the team observed the AUPRC vs. AUC-ROC divergence, suggesting post-hoc rationalization.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: File creation timestamp on the pre-analysis document predates training data extraction or model fitting by at least 30 days.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The pre-analysis document was written at the same time as data exploration where class imbalance was first observed, making it unclear if metric selection was informed by initial results.

must_not_claim: auprc_selection_is_cherry_picking_in_imbalanced_setting
auprc_is_niche_and_invalid_for_fraud_detection
model_should_be_rejected_for_not_using_auroc_as_primary_metric

suspicious_but_valid_features: Choosing AUPRC over AUC-ROC looks like non-standard metric selection or gaming, but at 0.23% fraud prevalence it is the methodologically correct primary metric. The conspicuously high AUC-ROC (0.963) actually validates why the team pre-specified AUPRC—it demonstrates the exact optimism artifact they were trying to avoid reporting as their headline number.
ground_truth_rationale: AUPRC is the recommended metric for severe class imbalance in fraud detection, a well-established position in the ML literature. The pre-specification six weeks before training eliminates the cherry-picking concern. The AUC-ROC of 0.963 at 0.23% prevalence is exactly the kind of inflated figure that motivated the metric choice.
notes: Pre-specified metric choice (rule 6). Red herring: AUPRC looks unusual but is correct. Easy case with clear documentary evidence.

task_prompt: A retail bank's customer analytics team built a customer churn prediction model using logistic regression to identify checking account holders likely to close their accounts within 90 days. The training dataset covers 24 months of account history across 95,000 customers. The feature set includes transaction frequency, average daily balance, number of products held, recency of last digital login, and age of account. A model reviewer noted that the team did not use a neural network or gradient boosting, claiming that 'logistic regression is too simple for this problem and the team should use XGBoost to maximize predictive performance.' The model achieved AUC-ROC of 0.779. The team's model selection memo, written before training, explains their rationale for the architecture choice: 'We are choosing logistic regression because (1) the bank's model risk management policy requires interpretable models for customer-impact decisions, (2) regulatory examiners require that churn-triggered outreach decisions be explainable to individual customers upon request under UDAAP guidelines, and (3) a power analysis showed that our sample size and feature set provide sufficient statistical power to detect the target effect size at AUC ≥ 0.75 with logistic regression, which meets the business requirements.' The reviewer did not engage with the compliance rationale and reiterated the XGBoost recommendation. Evaluate whether the model architecture choice is justified by the stated constraints, and whether the reviewer's performance-maximization argument is valid given those constraints.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Logistic regression is the appropriate model choice given binding regulatory interpretability requirements and a pre-specified power analysis confirming it meets the performance target.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer claims logistic regression is too simple and XGBoost would perform better, but the team had pre-specified binding regulatory constraints requiring interpretability (UDAAP compliance and bank model risk policy) and a power analysis confirming logistic regression meets the AUC ≥ 0.75 target—optimizing for raw performance at the cost of compliance is not an option.

ideal_critique: The reviewer's performance critique ignores binding regulatory constraints that preclude black-box models for this use case.
The power analysis confirming AUC ≥ 0.75 is achievable with logistic regression provides a pre-specified adequacy standard that the model meets (0.779).
A legitimate disclosure is that more complex models might achieve higher AUC but are excluded by regulatory requirements, which should be documented.
ideal_defense: UDAAP and bank model risk management policies are binding constraints, not preferences—interpretability is a requirement, not a tradeoff.
The pre-specified power analysis confirmed logistic regression would achieve the required AUC ≥ 0.75 for this sample size, and the model achieved 0.779.
Choosing a simpler model that meets regulatory requirements over a complex model that does not is correct model governance practice.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The regulatory interpretation is shown to be incorrect—i.e., UDAAP does not actually require the level of interpretability the team cited—and XGBoost with SHAP would satisfy the same requirements.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The bank's model risk management policy and legal counsel confirm that logistic regression (or equivalent interpretable models) is required for customer-impact outreach decisions.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Model risk management policy is ambiguous about whether SHAP-explained gradient boosting would satisfy interpretability requirements.

must_not_claim: logistic_regression_is_always_inadequate_for_churn_prediction
team_should_switch_to_xgboost_regardless_of_regulatory_constraints
auc_0.779_is_insufficient_for_this_use_case

suspicious_but_valid_features: Choosing logistic regression over gradient boosting looks like a performance-capping mistake, but regulatory and interpretability constraints make it the only compliant choice. The pre-specified power analysis confirming the model meets the AUC target removes any concern that the simple model was inadequate for the task.
ground_truth_rationale: Regulatory requirements and internal model governance policies are binding constraints that legitimately restrict model architecture choices. The power analysis pre-specified that logistic regression meets the performance bar (AUC ≥ 0.75), and the model delivers 0.779. The reviewer's argument ignores the compliance context entirely.
notes: Power analysis + regulatory constraints justify model choice (rules 6 and 7). Easy case. Tests whether evaluator understands compliance-constrained model selection.

task_prompt: A radiology AI company developed a deep learning model to triage chest X-rays for potential pneumothorax, flagging high-probability cases for urgent radiologist review. The model was trained on 210,000 chest X-rays from three academic hospital systems and achieved AUC-ROC of 0.952 on a held-out test set of 18,000 images drawn from those same three systems. An external reviewer raised a concern: 'The training data includes images from hospitals that use different X-ray acquisition protocols (CR vs. DR) and different PACS systems. This hardware heterogeneity introduces confounding variation and invalidates comparisons across hospital sites, because image characteristics differ systematically by acquisition type and these systematic differences could be spuriously learned as predictive features.' The team's data collection protocol, written before any data was assembled, explicitly states: 'We will intentionally include multiple acquisition hardware types (CR and DR) and multiple hospital PACS configurations to improve model generalization. We anticipate heterogeneous image quality as a feature of the training distribution, not a confound, consistent with our deployment target of diverse hospital environments.' Internal ablation experiments, conducted before finalizing the training set, showed that models trained only on same-hardware data generalized significantly worse to new hospital sites (AUC dropped to 0.887 on a cross-site test), while the heterogeneous training set maintained AUC 0.944 on the same cross-site test. Assess the validity of the reviewer's confounding concern.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Hardware heterogeneity in training data was deliberately designed to improve cross-site generalization, confirmed by pre-analysis ablations, making the reviewer's confounding concern invalid for the stated deployment scope.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer treats hardware variation as a confound, but the team deliberately included heterogeneous hardware to match their diverse deployment environment—pre-analysis ablations empirically demonstrated this improved cross-site generalization, validating the design choice.

ideal_critique: Hardware heterogeneity as a training strategy is only valid if the deployment environment is similarly heterogeneous, which the team's protocol confirms.
The pre-analysis ablation empirically validates the design: homogeneous training hurt cross-site generalization (AUC 0.887 vs. 0.944).

A legitimate disclosure is that the model's performance in any specific hardware-homogeneous deployment environment should be monitored, as the diverse training set may introduce some site-specific variance.
ideal_defense: Intentional data heterogeneity is a feature when deployment spans diverse environments—this is a well-established principle in domain generalization.
The pre-analysis ablation empirically demonstrated that homogeneous training was inferior for the cross-site deployment target.
The data collection protocol pre-specified this design intent before any data was collected, ruling out post-hoc rationalization.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The deployment environment is actually a single hardware-homogeneous hospital, in which case the heterogeneous training set would not be needed and same-hardware data would suffice.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Deployment is confirmed to span multiple hospital systems with mixed CR/DR hardware, matching the training distribution design.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Deployment scope has not been finalized and could include either single-site or multi-site installations.

must_not_claim: hardware_heterogeneity_always_confounds_medical_imaging_models
model_trained_on_mixed_hardware_is_methodologically_unsound
cr_dr_mixing_invalidates_performance_claims

suspicious_but_valid_features: Training on mixed CR and DR hardware from multiple PACS systems looks like uncontrolled confounding, but it was deliberately pre-specified to match the diverse deployment environment. The pre-analysis ablation provides empirical validation that heterogeneous training improves, not harms, cross-site generalization.
ground_truth_rationale: Deliberately including hardware heterogeneity in training data is a valid and recommended strategy for models intended for diverse deployment environments. The pre-analysis ablation provides empirical evidence that this choice improved the target outcome (cross-site AUC). The protocol pre-specification confirms this was not post-hoc rationalization.
notes: Empirical pre-analysis justification (rule 7). Red herring: hardware mixing looks like confounding but is intentional design. Medium difficulty: requires understanding of domain generalization.

task_prompt: A recommendation system team at a streaming media company built a ranking model to predict 7-day content completion rate—whether a user finishes a show within 7 days of starting it. The model uses gradient boosting on user engagement features: prior completion rates, genre preferences, device type, time-of-day viewing patterns, and session length history. Training data covers 6 months of user behavior from 4.2 million users, representing approximately 180 million viewing events. The team evaluated the model offline using normalized discounted cumulative gain (nDCG@10) and reported nDCG@10 = 0.81. A product reviewer observed that the training and evaluation data were collected during a period that included a major streaming catalog expansion occurring in Month 4 of the 6-month window, adding approximately 800 new titles including several high-profile exclusive releases. The reviewer argued that this 'regime change invalidates the temporal consistency assumption, rendering all reported metrics unreliable because the catalog and user behavior distributions fundamentally changed mid-window.' The team's analysis log shows they conducted a pre-training data audit identifying the catalog expansion event, then tested whether nDCG@10 differed materially between the pre-expansion (Months 1–3) and post-expansion (Months 4–6) evaluation periods. The pre-expansion nDCG was 0.80 and post-expansion nDCG was 0.82. The team documented: 'The catalog expansion did not materially affect model performance metrics; we will proceed with the full 6-month period as planned.' Assess whether the regime-change concern invalidates the reported metrics.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The team empirically tested for regime-change effects before finalizing the evaluation and found no material difference, making the invalidation claim unfounded.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer claims the catalog expansion invalidates all metrics, but the team proactively ran a pre-analysis test comparing metrics across pre- and post-expansion periods and found no material difference (0.80 vs. 0.82 nDCG@10)—empirically refuting the concern before it was raised.

ideal_critique: The regime-change concern is reasonable to raise, but the team already tested it empirically before selecting the evaluation window.
The pre-analysis test showing 0.80 vs. 0.82 nDCG@10 across the two sub-periods is the correct way to assess whether a distributional shift materially affects metrics.
A legitimate disclosure is that very large catalog expansions or content strategy changes could create larger regime shifts in future, warranting periodic re-evaluation.
ideal_defense: The team identified the catalog expansion event during data auditing and empirically tested its effect before finalizing the evaluation design.
A 0.02 nDCG@10 difference between pre- and post-expansion periods is within typical measurement variance and does not constitute a material regime change.
Using the full 6-month period after empirically confirming stability is the correct and data-efficient decision.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The catalog expansion disproportionately affected specific user segments or content types in ways that aggregate nDCG masks, creating hidden performance heterogeneity.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Segment-level analysis confirms nDCG stability across user cohorts and content categories both before and after the catalog expansion.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The catalog expansion heavily skewed toward genres that were underrepresented in the training data, creating a potential cold-start bias the aggregate metric cannot detect.

must_not_claim: any_regime_change_during_training_period_invalidates_all_metrics
catalog_expansion_necessarily_violates_iid_assumption
team_should_have_excluded_post_expansion_data

suspicious_but_valid_features: Including data spanning a major catalog expansion looks like a temporal consistency violation, but the team proactively ran a pre-analysis comparison across the two periods and found no material difference. The empirical test—not just theoretical reasoning—refutes the regime-change concern.
ground_truth_rationale: A regime-change concern is empirically falsifiable, and the team tested it before finalizing the evaluation design. The 0.02 nDCG@10 difference is within normal variance. The pre-analysis audit converts a potential flaw into a documented, evidence-backed design choice.
notes: Empirical pre-analysis justification (rule 7). Tests whether evaluator recognizes that empirically tested and dismissed concerns are different from uninvestigated concerns. Medium difficulty.

task_prompt: A mortgage lending company developed a model to predict whether a loan application will result in a completed closing within 45 days of approval. The model uses features derived from the loan file: loan-to-value ratio, borrower FICO, debt-to-income ratio, property type, lock rate versus market rate differential, loan officer tenure, and number of document resubmissions. The model is a logistic regression trained on 58,000 completed loan applications (those that eventually either closed or were withdrawn/declined after approval). A fair lending compliance reviewer raised a concern: 'Loan officer tenure is a proxy for race and gender because certain demographic groups are disproportionately assigned to junior loan officers. Including this feature may cause disparate impact.' The team consulted with their fair lending counsel and conducted a pre-deployment disparate impact analysis as required by their model risk policy. The analysis showed no statistically significant difference in loan officer tenure feature contribution across race or gender groups in the applicant pool after controlling for loan complexity. The legal analysis memo states: 'Loan officer tenure is operationally relevant to closing timeline prediction and does not demonstrate disparate impact in this dataset; inclusion is approved.' Evaluate whether the fair lending concern warrants removal of the feature.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Loan officer tenure was empirically tested for disparate impact by legal counsel using the required pre-deployment analysis and found non-significant, making removal unwarranted based on the stated concern.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer flags loan officer tenure as a potential racial/gender proxy, which is a legitimate concern in fair lending, but the team conducted the required pre-deployment disparate impact analysis with legal counsel and found no statistically significant disparate impact after controlling for loan complexity—validating feature retention.

ideal_critique: The disparate impact concern for loan officer tenure is legitimate to raise and was appropriately tested before deployment.
The legal analysis memo providing pre-deployment approval is the correct procedural outcome for a potentially sensitive feature.
A legitimate ongoing disclosure is that disparate impact should be re-tested periodically as the applicant pool or officer assignment patterns may change.
ideal_defense: Raising fair lending concerns for proxy variables is appropriate; the team followed the correct process of conducting a pre-deployment disparate impact analysis with legal counsel.
The analysis found no statistically significant disparate impact after controlling for loan complexity, providing empirical support for feature retention.
Loan officer tenure is genuinely predictive of closing timeline and its removal would decrease model utility without a demonstrated legal necessity.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The disparate impact analysis failed to control for loan complexity or used an inadequate sample size that lacked statistical power to detect moderate disparate impact effects.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The analysis is confirmed to use adequate statistical power and control for loan complexity, with p-values and effect sizes reported for key demographic groups.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The analysis shows borderline significance (p = 0.04–0.10) for one demographic group but not others, requiring judgment about whether this meets the threshold for disparate impact.

must_not_claim: loan_officer_tenure_must_be_removed_due_to_proxy_discrimination_regardless_of_impact_analysis
any_feature_correlated_with_demographics_is_per_se_discriminatory
disparate_impact_analysis_is_insufficient_to_clear_a_proxy_variable

suspicious_but_valid_features: Loan officer tenure as a model feature looks like a demographic proxy variable that could cause disparate impact, and this concern is worth raising—but the team ran the required pre-deployment disparate impact analysis with legal counsel and found no significant disparate impact after controlling for loan complexity, providing evidence-backed clearance for feature retention.
ground_truth_rationale: Fair lending analysis of proxy variables is the correct procedural response to disparate impact concerns, not automatic feature removal. The team followed the required process, obtained legal analysis, and found no significant disparate impact. The concern is procedurally resolved, not ignored. Ongoing monitoring remains a disclosure item.
notes: Empirical pre-analysis (disparate impact analysis) justifies feature inclusion (rule 7). Red herring: proxy variable concern is legitimate but was properly tested. Medium difficulty: requires understanding of fair lending compliance process.

task_prompt: A clinical decision support team developed a sepsis early-warning model for use in ICU settings at academic medical centers. The model uses a gradient-boosted tree trained on 4 years of ICU admission data from two large academic medical centers (87,000 patient-stays, 9.1% sepsis incidence). Features include vital sign trends (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure trajectories), lab value trajectories (lactate, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin), and nursing documentation flags for altered mental status and fluid balance. The model achieves AUC-ROC of 0.861 on a held-out test set from a third academic medical center not included in training. A clinical informaticist reviewer raised two concerns. First: 'The model was tested only at academic medical centers and cannot be claimed to generalize to community hospitals, where patient populations, staffing patterns, and clinical workflows differ substantially.' Second: 'Lactate is not available within 2 hours in many community hospital labs due to send-out testing arrangements, so the model's reliance on serum lactate makes it operationally invalid for those settings regardless of predictive performance.' The team's model documentation explicitly states the validated deployment scope as 'academic and large tertiary-care hospitals with rapid lactate turnaround (less than 2 hours), and the model should not be deployed in settings where lactate turnaround exceeds 2 hours without separate validation.' The documentation includes a feature importance analysis showing lactate contributes 14% of model information gain. Evaluate whether the reviewer's two concerns constitute invalidating methodological flaws or disclosure-appropriate scope limitations.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Both reviewer concerns describe scope limitations that are explicitly disclosed in the model documentation, not methodological flaws that invalidate performance claims within the stated scope.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer argues that academic-center-only testing and lactate dependency are invalidating flaws, but both are explicitly disclosed scope limitations in the model documentation—the model makes no claim to generalize beyond academic/tertiary care settings with rapid lactate, making these disclosure-appropriate limitations, not methodological errors.

ideal_critique: The two reviewer concerns are valid scope limitations that must be disclosed—and they already are in the model documentation.
The distinction between 'invalidating flaw' and 'disclosure-appropriate limitation' is critical: the model's claims are scoped to settings where the concerns do not apply.
A legitimate additional disclosure: even within academic centers, lactate turnaround time should be periodically audited to confirm it meets the < 2-hour requirement.
Deployment teams must implement guardrails preventing use in settings where lactate turnaround exceeds 2 hours.
ideal_defense: The model documentation explicitly restricts the validated deployment scope to academic and large tertiary-care hospitals with rapid lactate turnaround, precisely addressing both reviewer concerns.
A limitation that is disclosed and operationalized as a deployment restriction does not invalidate the model's performance claims within the stated scope.
The lactate feature importance (14%) warrants disclosure—which was provided—but does not invalidate the model if the deployment environment has rapid lactate availability.
Community hospital generalizability requires separate validation, which the team states explicitly; this is the correct position.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The model is being deployed or considered for deployment in community hospitals or settings without confirmed rapid lactate turnaround, in which case the scope limitation becomes an active deployment error.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Deployment is confirmed to be limited to academic and large tertiary-care centers with verified < 2-hour lactate turnaround, consistent with stated model scope.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model has been requested for use at a mixed-acuity hospital that sometimes has rapid lactate turnaround and sometimes does not, depending on lab staffing.

must_not_claim: academic_center_only_testing_invalidates_all_performance_claims
any_feature_unavailable_in_some_settings_invalidates_the_model
lactate_dependency_makes_the_model_methodologically_unsound

suspicious_but_valid_features: Testing exclusively at academic medical centers and relying on lactate—a test not always available rapidly in community hospitals—looks like major generalizability failures. However, the model explicitly scopes itself to academic and large tertiary-care settings with rapid lactate turnaround. Within that scope, the validation is sound and the concerns are disclosed limitations with operational deployment guardrails, not methodological invalidators.
ground_truth_rationale: The critical evaluator skill in this hard case is distinguishing a limitation that warrants disclosure from one that invalidates claims. Both concerns are real scope limitations—and they are explicitly disclosed. The model makes no claim about community hospital performance. Within the disclosed scope, the methodology is sound. The reviewer conflates 'not universally applicable' with 'methodologically invalid,' which is the error to identify.
notes: Hard case: requires distinguishing disclosure-appropriate limitation from invalidating flaw (rule 8). Domain-specific knowledge needed: clinical lab turnaround, ICU informatics (rule at batch level). Tests the critical distinction between scope limitation and methodology error.

task_prompt: A consumer lender's model team built a machine learning model for thin-file credit applicants—people with fewer than 5 trade lines on their credit bureau report. The model uses alternative data sources: rental payment history (from a rent reporting bureau), utility payment history, bank account cash flow patterns, and employment verification data. The model was trained on 31,000 thin-file applicants who were approved under a pre-existing human underwriting program, with 18-month loan performance observed. The model achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.791. A model risk reviewer raised two concerns: (1) 'The training data is restricted to approved applicants, introducing survivorship bias that will cause the model to overestimate creditworthiness of the applicant population'; and (2) 'Alternative data sources like rent payment history and utility payments are not regulated under the Fair Credit Reporting Act in the same way as bureau tradelines, creating legal and model risk.' On concern (1), the team's model risk memo acknowledges: 'The approved-applicant-only training set introduces sample selection bias; however, this model is designed to function as a second-stage filter applied only after the same pre-existing human underwriting criteria are applied, meaning the model will only score applicants who have already passed human underwriting—the population the model was trained on.' Evaluate both concerns and whether they warrant rejection of the model.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Survivorship bias is a disclosed limitation that is operationally mitigated by constraining deployment to the same filtered applicant population, and alternative data compliance is a deployment governance issue, not a modeling error.

planted_issues.description: The survivorship bias concern is real but operationally mitigated: the model is deployed only to applicants who pass the same human underwriting filter as the training population, so the model sees the same distribution it was trained on. The alternative data legal concern is a deployment compliance question, not a modeling methodology error—validating both concerns as disclosed limitations rather than invalidating flaws.

ideal_critique: The survivorship bias concern is legitimate but resolved by the deployment constraint: the model scores only applicants who pass the same human underwriting criteria, matching the training distribution.
If the human underwriting criteria were ever relaxed, the model would be exposed to an out-of-distribution population and would need retraining—this should be a documented dependency.

The alternative data legal concern (FCRA status of rent and utility data) is a valid compliance issue that must be addressed by the legal and compliance team before deployment, but it does not affect modeling methodology.
The model risk reviewer should route concern (2) to the compliance and legal team, not use it to reject the model on methodological grounds.
ideal_defense: Survivorship bias is a named and acknowledged limitation; its practical impact is neutralized by deploying the model only to the same filtered population.
The deployment architecture—model as a second-stage filter after human underwriting—is explicitly documented and creates an operational control that matches training and scoring distributions.

Alternative data legality is a compliance and procurement question; the team should demonstrate FCRA compliance for alternative data sources as a deployment gate, but this is separate from whether the model itself is methodologically sound.
Both concerns are appropriately handled: one is operationally mitigated, the other is routed to compliance.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The human underwriting criteria are not codified as a hard deployment gate, meaning the model could be applied to applicants who did not pass human underwriting—violating the distribution-matching assumption.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: System architecture documentation confirms the model is technically constrained to score only applicants who have passed the human underwriting filter, enforced by a pipeline gate.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The human underwriting filter exists as a process step but is not enforced as a technical deployment gate, creating operational risk that it could be bypassed.

must_not_claim: approved_applicant_training_always_invalidates_credit_models
alternative_data_sources_are_per_se_invalid_for_credit_models
survivorship_bias_cannot_be_operationally_mitigated_by_deployment_constraints

suspicious_but_valid_features: Training only on approved applicants (survivorship bias) and using alternative data with uncertain FCRA status both look like serious model-invalidating problems. However, the survivorship bias is operationally neutralized by restricting scoring to the same filtered population, and the alternative data legal question is a compliance process issue external to the modeling methodology. Both concerns are real but resolved appropriately—one by deployment architecture and one by routing to the correct team.
ground_truth_rationale: This hard case requires distinguishing a real-but-mitigated limitation from an invalidating flaw. Survivorship bias in training data is real; its materiality depends on whether deployment respects the same sample filter—and here, the deployment architecture enforces that constraint. The alternative data concern is a legitimate compliance issue but is not a modeling error. Both are disclosure-appropriate limitations with specified mitigations.
notes: Hard case: requires distinguishing disclosure-appropriate limitation from invalidating flaw (rule 8). Domain knowledge: thin-file credit, alternative data, FCRA, sample selection bias in credit scoring. Tests dual-concern resolution.

task_prompt: A large commercial bank's quantitative risk team built an internal credit rating model for mid-market commercial loans (loan sizes $5M–$100M). The model combines financial statement ratios (leverage, coverage, liquidity), industry sector dummies, geographic region, and loan officer qualitative assessment scores into a logistic regression predicting probability of default within 3 years. The training set covers 12,400 loan originations from 2010–2020 with observed default outcomes. The model is calibrated to produce probability of default (PD) estimates aligned to the bank's historical default rate by rating grade. Two concerns are raised by a model validator. First: 'The 2010–2020 training window includes the post-financial-crisis recovery period, which is characterized by unusually low default rates. This creates an optimistic bias in PD estimates.' Second: 'Using loan officer qualitative assessment scores as features introduces human judgment that may be biased and unauditable.' Regarding concern 1, the team's model documentation includes a stress-testing supplement showing PD calibration under a stressed macroeconomic scenario (using 2007–2009 historical default rates applied as an overlay), which is explicitly reported separately from base-case PD estimates and used for capital adequacy calculations. The documentation states the base-case PD reflects benign cycle conditions, with stress PDs provided for capital purposes. Evaluate both concerns.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The benign-cycle PD concern is disclosed and operationally handled via a separate stress-PD supplement used for capital purposes; the loan officer score concern is a governance question requiring audit trails but does not invalidate the modeling approach.

planted_issues.description: The validator flags low default rates in the 2010–2020 training window as causing optimistic PD bias, but the team's documentation explicitly discloses this as a benign-cycle base case and provides separate stress PDs for capital adequacy—the concern is a disclosure requirement already satisfied, not an unaddressed flaw.

ideal_critique: The benign-cycle concern is valid: base-case PDs calibrated to 2010–2020 will underestimate through-the-cycle default rates—but this is explicitly disclosed and addressed by the stress PD supplement.
Using base-case PD for day-to-day credit decisions and stress PD for capital is standard Basel-compliant practice.
The loan officer score concern raises a legitimate model governance question: scoring subjectivity requires audit trails, calibration checks, and periodic interrater reliability testing.
The loan officer score concern does not invalidate the model but requires governance controls as a deployment condition.
ideal_defense: Dual PD reporting (base-case and stress) is the Basel III-aligned standard for internal rating-based (IRB) credit models.
The stress supplement applies historically observed recession-era default rates as an overlay, providing a conservative bound on PD estimates for capital adequacy.
Loan officer qualitative scores are common inputs in commercial credit models; their inclusion is appropriate if governance controls (score calibration, audit trails) are in place.
Neither concern invalidates the model's methodology; both are addressed by disclosure, governance, or regulatory-aligned dual reporting.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The bank uses only base-case PDs (not stress PDs) for regulatory capital calculations, which would be non-compliant and would make the benign-cycle concern a material unaddressed risk.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Stress PDs from the supplement are confirmed to be used for capital adequacy and regulatory reporting, consistent with Basel III IRB requirements.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: It is unclear whether the stress PD supplement is formally integrated into the capital calculation workflow or is a standalone document not connected to regulatory reporting.

must_not_claim: post_crisis_training_window_always_invalidates_credit_rating_models
loan_officer_score_features_are_per_se_invalid_in_credit_models
model_must_be_retrained_on_full_economic_cycle_data_to_be_valid

suspicious_but_valid_features: Training on a post-crisis recovery period and including subjective loan officer scores both look like serious methodological problems. However, the benign-cycle PD issue is disclosed and addressed via a separate stress-PD supplement used for capital adequacy—a standard Basel III IRB practice. The loan officer score concern is a model governance question (requiring audit trails) rather than a modeling error. Both concerns require acknowledgment but neither invalidates the model.
ground_truth_rationale: Commercial credit rating models under Basel IRB frameworks routinely use point-in-time and through-the-cycle PD estimates separately; the dual-reporting approach is the regulatory standard, not a workaround. Loan officer qualitative inputs are common in commercial credit models and are governed by interrater reliability requirements. Hard case: evaluator must know Basel IRB credit model standards and distinguish governance requirements from modeling invalidators.
notes: Hard case requiring domain-specific knowledge: Basel III IRB credit model requirements, through-the-cycle vs. point-in-time PD (rule at batch level re: domain-specific knowledge). Distinguishes disclosure-appropriate limitation from invalidating flaw (rule 8).

task_prompt: An insurance company's actuarial and data science team built a gradient-boosted model to predict individual health insurance claim costs for ACA marketplace plans. The model is used to inform prospective premium pricing for the upcoming plan year and to set internal risk reserves. Features include age, region, prior-year claims (for renewing members), diagnosis code categories, prescription drug tier usage, and benefit plan type. The model is trained on 3 years of paid claims data (280,000 member-years). A model review committee raised two concerns. First: 'Age and region are included as features, but age and geographic rating area are the only rating factors permitted under ACA; using them as ML features alongside other factors may constitute rating on impermissible factors in a way that violates actuarial soundness requirements.' Second: 'Prior-year claims is a proxy for health status, which is an impermissible rating factor under ACA for community rating purposes.' The team's actuarial memo, reviewed by outside counsel, explains: 'This model is used for internal cost prediction and reserve setting, not for determining individual premium rates charged to members. ACA community rating rules govern member-facing premium rates, which are set separately using only permissible rating factors. The cost prediction model informs risk segmentation for actuarial reserve accuracy and reinsurance assessment, not individual pricing.' Evaluate both regulatory concerns.

ground_truth.final_verdict: ACA community rating restrictions apply to member-facing premium rates, not to internal actuarial cost prediction models used for reserves and reinsurance; both regulatory concerns are misdirected to the wrong model use case.

planted_issues.description: The reviewer applies ACA community rating restrictions to an internal cost prediction model, but those restrictions govern member-facing premium rates, not internal actuarial reserves or reinsurance pricing—outside counsel confirmed this distinction, making both regulatory concerns misdirected.

ideal_critique: The ACA community rating concern would be valid if the model outputs were used to set individual member premiums, but the actuarial memo confirmed this model is used for reserves and reinsurance, not member pricing.
A legitimate disclosure: the team must maintain strict operational separation between the cost prediction model's outputs and any member-facing pricing process to prevent regulatory risk.
If the model's risk segmentation outputs were ever used to de facto influence which members are targeted for plan changes, that could create indirect ACA compliance risk.
Outside counsel review is the appropriate mechanism for ACA regulatory questions, and their clearance should be documented.
ideal_defense: ACA Section 2701 community rating restrictions apply to premium rates charged to members, not to internal actuarial modeling or reinsurance assessment.
Internal cost prediction models using health status and prior claims are standard actuarial practice for reserve adequacy and reinsurance pricing.
Outside counsel reviewed and confirmed the regulatory scope distinction in the actuarial memo.
The model and the member-facing rating process are operationally separated, addressing the indirect use concern.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The model's risk segmentation outputs are shown to be used as inputs into the member-facing premium determination process, collapsing the operational separation the team claims.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Audit of data flows confirms that model outputs feed only into reserve calculations and reinsurance contracts, with no path to individual member premium determination.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The risk segmentation outputs inform benefit design decisions (e.g., plan formulary changes) that differentially affect high-risk members, creating indirect health-status-based impact.

must_not_claim: using_health_status_features_in_any_aca_health_insurance_model_violates_community_rating
prior_year_claims_cannot_be_used_in_aca_health_insurance_ml_models
age_and_region_features_in_an_internal_model_violate_aca_rating_rules

suspicious_but_valid_features: Using age, region, prior-year claims, and diagnosis code categories as features in an ACA health insurance model looks like a clear community rating violation. However, ACA Section 2701 restrictions apply to member-facing premium rates, not internal actuarial cost models. Outside counsel confirmed this scope distinction. The model is correctly scoped to reserves and reinsurance, where health status features are standard actuarial inputs.
ground_truth_rationale: This case requires domain-specific knowledge of ACA actuarial and regulatory structure: community rating restrictions (Section 2701) apply to individual member premium rates, not internal actuarial models. Internal cost prediction models using health status are standard and legally distinct from rating practices. Both regulatory concerns are incorrectly applied to the wrong model function. Hard case: requires insurance regulatory and actuarial knowledge.
notes: Hard case requiring domain-specific knowledge: ACA Section 2701 community rating rules, actuarial reserve modeling vs. premium rating (rule at batch level). Distinguishes disclosure-appropriate limitation from invalidating flaw (rule 8). Outside counsel review is the pre-analysis equivalent.

task_prompt: A property and casualty insurance company developed a machine learning model to predict the probability that a homeowners insurance policy will generate a claim within 12 months of inception. The model uses home characteristics (age of structure, construction type, square footage, roof material and age), location features (FEMA flood zone designation, state wildfire risk score, local crime index), and applicant credit score as inputs. The model was trained on 5 years of policy data across 340,000 policies written by the company. The insurtech team proposes using the model score to automatically decline applications scoring in the top 10% risk decile, without routing those applications for manual underwriter review. The primary deployment state is a state in which the insurance commissioner's office has issued a bulletin clarifying that any automated adverse underwriting action must provide a 'specific reason' for the declination, stated in plain non-technical language that the applicant can understand and potentially dispute. The model is implemented as a gradient-boosted tree, which does not produce individual-level feature attributions by default; only global feature importance values are available. The team's technology lead suggests that providing the top global feature importance factors—'flood zone designation, roof material, credit score'—as boilerplate adverse action reasons for all automated declines in the top decile would satisfy the regulatory requirement without additional engineering work. Evaluate whether this deployment plan is technically compliant with the state regulatory requirement and whether the proposed adverse action approach is appropriate.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Using global feature importance as boilerplate adverse action reasons does not satisfy the regulatory requirement for individual-specific declination reasons and exposes the insurer to regulatory and legal risk.

planted_issues.description: State insurance regulations require specific reasons for adverse underwriting decisions; providing the same boilerplate global feature importance list to all declined applicants does not constitute an individual-specific reason and likely violates state insurance code adverse action notice requirements.

A gradient-boosted tree without an explanation layer (SHAP or equivalent) cannot produce individual-level feature attributions; the team's proposed workaround using global importance does not meet the individual specificity requirement.

ideal_critique: State insurance adverse action notice requirements demand individual-specific reasons, not global model statistics.
Global feature importance reflects model-level behavior and cannot explain why a specific applicant was declined; SHAP values or equivalent individual explanations are required.
Deploying without an individual explanation capability creates regulatory enforcement risk and potential policyholder litigation.
The correct remediation is to add SHAP or LIME-based individual explanation capability before deploying automated adverse action.
ideal_defense: The model accurately identifies high-risk properties, and automated declination is a common industry practice.
The proposed global feature importance reasons are transparent about the factors driving model decisions at the population level.
Credit score adverse action reasons under FCRA provide a precedent for factor-based adverse action notices.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: State insurance commissioner review confirms that boilerplate global feature importance does not satisfy individual adverse action notice requirements.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: State insurance regulator issues formal guidance that population-level factor disclosure satisfies adverse action notice requirements for ML-based underwriting.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: State insurance code is silent on the level of specificity required, and the regulator has not issued ML-specific adverse action guidance.

must_not_claim: credit_score_cannot_be_used_in_homeowners_insurance_underwriting
gradient_boosting_is_inherently_noncompliant_for_insurance_underwriting
automated_declination_is_prohibited_for_homeowners_insurance

ground_truth_rationale: The adverse action notice requirement is individual-specific; global feature importance is a model-population statistic, not an individual explanation. This is a deployment compliance failure, not a modeling performance issue. The FCRA credit score analogy is inapt because FCRA specifies its own adverse action framework that does not apply to insurance underwriting.
notes: Deployment decision with clear regulatory compliance failure. Easy case. Tests whether evaluator distinguishes individual vs. population-level explanation requirements.

task_prompt: A regional health system's clinical informatics team trained a 30-day unplanned readmission prediction model on 4 years of discharge records (82,000 discharges, 11.4% readmission rate) from their three hospitals. The model, a gradient-boosted tree, achieved AUC-ROC of 0.76 on a held-out test set comprising the most recent 12 months of data. The health system's CMO proposes deploying the model to automatically trigger care coordination calls for all patients scoring above the 80th percentile risk threshold at discharge. Each care coordination call costs $45 per patient. Readmissions cost the health system approximately $12,000 each (net of revenue received), and the health system participates in the CMS Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP), which applies penalty adjustments to Medicare payments based on aggregate risk-adjusted readmission rates for specific high-volume diagnoses including heart failure, pneumonia, COPD, and hip/knee replacement. The model was evaluated exclusively on historical data from the same three hospitals; there is no external validation at a community hospital or safety-net setting. The health system recently completed an acquisition of a safety-net hospital serving a predominantly low-income population with substantially different payer mix, comorbidity burden, and social determinants of health compared to the existing three hospitals. The safety-net hospital is expected to be added to the deployment footprint six months after the planned model launch. The 80th percentile threshold was selected based on operational capacity for care coordination calls, not on a cost-benefit analysis of the intervention. Evaluate whether the proposed deployment is ready to proceed given these circumstances.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The deployment plan has a critical gap: the safety-net hospital merger creates a near-term distribution shift with no validation plan, and asymmetric error costs have not been incorporated into threshold selection.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Prospective pilot at the safety-net hospital for 90 days with performance monitoring against a held-out calibration set from that site before full deployment.

planted_issues.description: The model was validated on three academic hospitals; the safety-net hospital has different patient demographics, payer mix, and social determinants of health that could materially degrade model performance within six months of deployment.

The 80th percentile threshold was selected without a formal cost-benefit analysis; given the $12,000 readmission cost versus $45 intervention cost, the cost-optimal threshold is likely lower than the 80th percentile, meaning the current threshold leaves high-value interventions unrealized.

HRRP penalty calculations aggregate readmission rates across the health system; the safety-net hospital's higher baseline readmission rate will affect system-wide penalty adjustments in ways the current model evaluation does not account for.

ideal_critique: The imminent safety-net hospital merger creates a near-term distribution shift that is unvalidated; deploying without a site-specific validation plan is a material risk.

The 80th percentile threshold was not derived from the cost asymmetry; with a $12,000 readmission cost and $45 intervention cost, the cost-optimal threshold should be derived from the ROC curve using the cost ratio.
HRRP penalty structure creates an incentive to reduce readmissions at penalty-bearing DRGs; the model should be evaluated on whether it identifies those specific high-penalty readmission types.
Retrospective validation on historical data from three hospitals does not constitute prospective validation for a new site with a different patient population.
ideal_defense: The AUC-ROC of 0.76 is acceptable for readmission prediction (comparable to published LACE+ and HOSPITAL scores).
The existing three-hospital validation is appropriate for the current deployment sites.
The $45 vs. $12,000 cost ratio strongly favors broad intervention, supporting deployment even at moderate model accuracy.
HRRP penalties provide additional institutional incentive to deploy proactively.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A 90-day pilot at the safety-net hospital shows AUC degradation > 0.05 or calibration failure (predicted risk systematically under- or over-estimated), confirming distribution shift.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Safety-net hospital pilot shows AUC within 0.03 of held-out test set performance and calibration curves remain accurate, indicating the model generalizes.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: AUC is maintained but calibration is off by a constant factor, suggesting the model ranks patients correctly but absolute risk estimates need recalibration.

must_not_claim: auc_0.76_is_too_low_for_any_readmission_prediction_use
retrospective_evaluation_is_never_sufficient_for_deployment_decisions
all_readmission_models_must_be_validated_at_community_hospitals_before_deployment

ground_truth_rationale: The critical flaw is the known, near-term distribution shift introduced by the safety-net hospital merger with no validation plan, combined with a threshold selection that ignores the known cost asymmetry. HRRP penalty externalities add a deployment context dimension. These are not generic readmission model critiques—they are specific to this deployment scenario.
notes: Real-world framing: deployment decision with cost asymmetry and distribution shift. Retrospective-to-prospective deployment framing. 3 must-find issues. Hard asymmetric error costs inferable from HRRP domain knowledge.

task_prompt: A national grocery retail chain operates 400 stores and built a demand forecasting model to optimize automated replenishment orders for perishable goods including produce, dairy, and bakery items. The model is a gradient-boosted tree using features: historical sales by SKU and store, local weather forecasts, promotional calendar flags, day-of-week and federal holiday indicators, store size tier, and regional demographic indices. It was trained on 2 years of daily sales data across all 400 stores, representing approximately 290 million store-SKU-day observations. Offline evaluation on a 60-day holdout shows MAPE of 8.3% across all stores. The supply chain leadership team proposes replacing the existing rule-based replenishment system entirely with the ML model, giving it full autonomy to set daily order quantities for all 400 stores and all perishable SKUs, with no human override capability built into the deployment architecture. The chain's stores have highly variable characteristics: urban flagship locations average 42,000 weekly transactions while the smallest rural stores average 3,100 weekly transactions. The bottom 50 stores by volume account for fewer than 3% of total chain sales. When the team analyzed holdout performance by store volume quintile, the model's MAPE on the bottom-quintile stores (lowest 20% by transaction volume) was 19.4%—more than double the overall MAPE of 8.3%. The model's performance on the top three quintiles ranged from 6.1% to 9.2% MAPE. Evaluate the proposed full-autonomy deployment across all 400 stores.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model is ready for high-volume store deployment but requires human override capability and separate treatment for low-volume stores where performance is materially degraded.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: A 30-day parallel run comparing ML-determined orders against human-reviewed replenishment decisions at low-volume stores, measuring waste rate and stockout frequency against the current system.

planted_issues.description: The 19.4% MAPE at low-volume stores is materially worse than the 8.3% aggregate MAPE; full-autonomy deployment at these stores will cause elevated waste and stockout rates that the aggregate metric conceals.

Full autonomy with no override capability means that model errors for perishables—which cannot be held over—translate directly to write-offs or stockouts with no correction pathway, creating one-directional irreversible errors.

ideal_critique: The aggregate MAPE of 8.3% masks the 19.4% MAPE at low-volume stores, where the model may not have enough training data to learn store-specific patterns.
Full-autonomy deployment for perishables without override capability means model errors convert directly to food waste or stockouts with no intervention path.
The model performs well enough for high-volume stores to justify deployment there, but low-volume stores require a separate treatment plan.
ideal_defense: 8.3% MAPE is competitive with published benchmarks for grocery demand forecasting.
The bottom 50 stores account for only 3% of total sales, so performance there has limited financial impact at the system level.
Full automation would eliminate the operational cost of human review across 400 stores.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: 30-day parallel run shows low-volume store waste and stockout rates are materially worse under ML orders than the current rule-based system.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Parallel run shows low-volume store waste and stockout rates are comparable to the current system, suggesting 19.4% MAPE is acceptable in operational context.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Waste rate improves but stockout rate worsens at low-volume stores, or vice versa, creating a tradeoff that requires business judgment.

must_not_claim: 8.3_percent_mape_is_insufficient_for_any_grocery_demand_forecasting_application
ml_models_cannot_be_used_for_autonomous_replenishment_decisions
gradient_boosting_is_inappropriate_for_demand_forecasting

ground_truth_rationale: The mixed verdict reflects that the model is genuinely suitable for high-volume stores but materially underperforms at low-volume stores. The key insight is that aggregate MAPE conceals segment-level performance gaps, and full-autonomy for perishables removes the error correction mechanism. Both concerns are specific to the deployment decision, not generic model critiques.
notes: Mixed position case (rule: at least 3 mixed). Full autonomy deployment decision for perishables. 2 must-find issues. Multiple acceptable resolutions.

task_prompt: A fintech lender deployed an automated loan underwriting model 14 months ago for personal installment loans with principal amounts between $2,000 and $25,000. The model uses verified income, applicant FICO credit score, total debt-to-income ratio, and employment tenure to generate a creditworthiness score that drives auto-approval, auto-decline, or manual review routing. The deployment team is now evaluating two proposed expansions of model authority: first, raising the auto-approval loan size ceiling from $25,000 to $50,000; and second, lowering the minimum FICO score threshold for auto-approval eligibility from 660 to 620. Both expansions are proposed on the basis of retrospective performance data: over the 14 months since deployment, auto-approved loans have shown a 12-month observed default rate of 3.1%, while manually underwritten loans in the same period—loans that were too large or too thin-file to qualify for auto-approval—showed a 12-month observed default rate of 3.4%. The deployment team interprets this as evidence that the model is a superior underwriter relative to human review, and argues this retrospective performance comparison justifies expanding model authority to higher loan amounts and lower credit quality borrowers. No shadow mode or champion-challenger testing was performed for the proposed expanded scopes prior to the proposal. Evaluate whether the retrospective comparison provides valid justification for the proposed scope expansion and whether the expansion plan is methodologically appropriate.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective default rate comparison does not support scope expansion because both expansions move into regimes materially different from the model's training and validation conditions, and the comparison confounds selection effects with model quality.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Prospective shadow underwriting of 1,000 loans in the $25,001–$50,000 range and 1,000 loans with FICO 620–659, using model scores with human review, for 6 months before any auto-approval expansion.

planted_issues.description: The model was trained and validated on loans up to $25,000 and FICO ≥ 660; expanding to $50,000 loans and FICO 620–659 moves into regimes where the model has no validated performance data and where default behavior and borrower characteristics are materially different.

The 3.1% vs. 3.4% default rate comparison confounds model quality with selection effects: model-approved loans are filtered by model risk assessment while manually underwritten loans include cases the model would have rejected, making this comparison invalid evidence for model superiority.

Expanding model authority without a champion-challenger or shadow-mode validation phase means any model failures at the expanded scope will cause real credit losses before performance issues can be detected and corrected.

ideal_critique: The retrospective performance comparison is confounded: auto-approved loans are filtered by the model's own score cutoff, while manual loans include model-rejected cases, making the comparison invalid as evidence of model superiority.
Expanding to $50,000 loans and FICO 620–659 moves beyond the validated training distribution; these regimes may have different default drivers not captured in the current feature set.
The expansion should use a shadow-mode or champion-challenger design to accumulate evidence before committing full auto-approval authority.
ideal_defense: The 14-month retrospective performance is strong evidence that the model generalizes well within its current scope.
The 3.1% default rate demonstrates model effectiveness; a 3.4% manual underwriting rate suggests model screening adds value.
Expanding model authority gradually is a legitimate business strategy when model performance has been demonstrated.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Shadow underwriting of expanded-scope loans shows default rates > 4.5% or materially worse calibration than the current auto-approved population, confirming out-of-distribution degradation.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Shadow underwriting shows comparable default rates (within 0.5 percentage points) and calibration accuracy for the expanded loan size and FICO range.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Default rates are within range for the FICO expansion but elevated for the loan size expansion, suggesting the two proposed changes have different risk profiles.

must_not_claim: retrospective_model_evaluation_is_never_valid_for_underwriting_decisions
fico_620_is_too_low_for_any_ml_loan_model
model_performance_below_3_percent_default_rate_is_always_acceptable_for_expansion

ground_truth_rationale: The retrospective comparison is not a valid test of model superiority because of selection confounding. The proposed expansions move into regimes with different training distribution, requiring prospective validation. The mixed verdict recognizes the model's current-scope performance is strong and the expansion critique is specific to the out-of-distribution regimes—not a general rejection of the model.
notes: Mixed position case. Retrospective-to-prospective deployment framing (rule: at least 2 such cases). 3 must-find issues. Tests understanding of selection confounding and out-of-distribution deployment.

task_prompt: A city transit authority partnered with a university research group to build a machine learning model predicting whether individual bus trips will arrive more than 15 minutes late—the threshold the authority defines as a 'significant delay' in its service quality standards—at 180 monitored bus stops across a mid-size metropolitan area. The model was trained on 18 months of archival GPS telemetry and operator delay records, along with scheduled headways, historical delay patterns by route and time-of-day, real-time traffic incident feeds, and NWS weather data. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.72 on a held-out test set for the significant-delay class. The transit authority's communications and digital products team proposes deploying the model's predictions in the passenger-facing mobile app, displaying the message 'Delay Alert: your bus is likely to be significantly delayed' whenever the model's predicted probability of significant delay exceeds 0.60. The alert threshold of 0.60 was chosen by the product team based on intuition about minimizing user complaints, without formal analysis of the precision-recall tradeoff or the behavioral consequences of false alerts. Transit authority survey data indicates that passengers who receive a delay alert frequently choose to take ride-hail services, walk, or wait for a subsequent bus rather than remaining at the stop. This mode-switching behavior affects actual ridership loads on the predicted bus run and potentially on subsequent runs on the same route. Evaluate the proposed passenger-facing deployment plan, including the threshold choice and the deployment architecture.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model has acceptable performance for operational use, but passenger-facing alert deployment requires threshold analysis accounting for behavioral response feedback and its effect on ridership and model calibration.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: A/B test of alert threshold variants (0.55, 0.60, 0.65) across randomly assigned stop clusters for 60 days, measuring false alert complaint rates, ridership change at alerted stops, and downstream model calibration stability.

planted_issues.description: Passengers who receive delay alerts change transport mode, which reduces ridership at the alerted stop and potentially alters actual bus arrival patterns—creating a feedback loop where the model's predictions influence the outcome they are predicting, degrading calibration over time.

The 0.60 threshold was not derived from analysis of the tradeoff between false alerts (which erode passenger trust and cause unnecessary mode switches) and missed delay warnings (which cause missed connections); for a passenger-facing system, false alert tolerance is a service design question, not a pure precision-recall optimization.

ideal_critique: Passenger-facing delay alerts that cause mode switching create a feedback loop: if enough passengers leave the alerted stop, the reduced load may change the bus's arrival time, altering the very outcome the model predicted.
The 0.60 threshold was chosen without analysis of false alert tolerance, which for passenger-facing systems is a service quality parameter, not just a statistical one.
The transit authority should model the behavioral elasticity of alert-triggered mode switching before deploying at scale.
ideal_defense: F1 = 0.72 is a reasonable performance level for a complex urban transit prediction problem.
Informing passengers about likely delays respects their autonomy to make alternative travel decisions.
The 0.60 threshold is a sensible starting point that can be adjusted after deployment based on feedback.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A/B test shows stops with alert deployment have statistically significant ridership decline that alters subsequent delay patterns, confirming the feedback loop disrupts model calibration.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A/B test shows ridership change at alerted stops is minimal (< 5% mode switch rate), and model calibration is stable across alert and no-alert conditions after 60 days.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Ridership decline is moderate but concentrated on specific routes or times, requiring route-specific threshold calibration rather than a universal fix.

must_not_claim: f1_0.72_is_insufficient_for_any_transit_prediction_application
passenger_facing_ml_predictions_require_perfect_precision_before_deployment
probabilistic_delay_alerts_are_inappropriate_for_public_transit_apps

ground_truth_rationale: The critical issue is the behavioral feedback loop: passenger responses to alerts change the outcome the model is predicting, creating a non-stationary environment. This is a deployment-specific concern that does not appear in the offline evaluation. Threshold selection for passenger-facing systems requires service design input beyond precision-recall tradeoff. Both issues are specific to the deployment decision.
notes: Mixed position case. Deployment decision with feedback loop dynamics. 2 must-find issues. Multiple acceptable resolutions.

task_prompt: An e-commerce company's data science team developed a real-time personalized product recommendation model using a two-tower neural network architecture, trained on 120 million user-item interaction events collected over 6 months. The model produces personalized product rankings for the homepage product carousel, which displays the top 20 recommended products to each logged-in user. Offline evaluation of the model shows recall@20 of 0.41 and NDCG@20 of 0.37, both improvements over the incumbent collaborative filtering model. The team ran a prospective online A/B test over 2 weeks, randomizing 5% of total traffic between the new model (treatment) and the incumbent model (control). The A/B test observed a statistically significant 2.1% lift in click-through rate (CTR) for the new model versus the incumbent (p < 0.001, 95% CI: [1.7%, 2.5%]). Product leadership proposes rolling out the new model to 100% of traffic immediately. The team's A/B analysis also contains the following observation about recommendation concentration: under the new model, 73% of homepage carousel impressions were served from just 8% of the total product catalog; under the incumbent model, the same top 8% of catalog items accounted for 55% of impressions. Items ranked 500th or lower in catalog popularity received 40% fewer impressions under the new model than under the incumbent. The company sells products from thousands of third-party merchants, and its standard merchant agreement guarantees merchants above a minimum revenue threshold a specified minimum impression share per quarter. Evaluate whether the proposed full production rollout is appropriate given all available information.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The CTR lift is real and the model is technically superior, but the concentration effect may breach merchant agreement impression guarantees and the long-tail suppression should be evaluated for revenue diversity impact before full rollout.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Audit of merchant impression guarantee compliance under new model rankings for all contracted merchants before full production deployment.

planted_issues.description: Third-party merchant contracts guarantee minimum impression shares; the new model's concentration of 73% of impressions on 8% of catalog items may breach these contractual guarantees for merchants in the long-tail, creating legal and commercial risk.

A 40% reduction in impressions for long-tail items may suppress discovery of new or niche products, reducing catalog health and merchant diversity over time in ways that aggregate CTR does not capture.

ideal_critique: The CTR lift is validated by a well-designed A/B test and is likely real.
However, the impression concentration must be audited against merchant agreement guarantees before rollout—potential contractual breach is not captured in CTR.
Long-tail suppression may reduce catalog diversity and new-product discovery, which are strategic business concerns not reflected in short-term CTR.
A constrained ranking approach (ensuring minimum merchant impression shares while maximizing CTR) would address the business constraint without sacrificing model lift.
ideal_defense: The 2.1% CTR lift is statistically significant with p < 0.001 and was measured in a properly designed A/B test.
Concentration on popular items reflects genuine user preferences; the model is accurately modeling user intent.
Recall@20 of 0.41 and NDCG@20 of 0.37 are strong offline metrics for homepage recommendations.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Merchant impression guarantee audit finds that ≥ 5% of contracted merchants fall below guaranteed impression thresholds under new model rankings, confirming breach risk.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Merchant impression audit shows all contracted merchants meet guaranteed minimums under new model rankings, and CTR improvement holds across merchant tiers.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Most merchants meet impression guarantees but 2–3 high-revenue merchants fall slightly below threshold, requiring negotiation rather than model redesign.

must_not_claim: 2.1_percent_ctr_lift_is_too_small_to_justify_model_deployment
two_tower_neural_networks_are_inappropriate_for_e_commerce_recommendations
a_b_test_results_are_insufficient_to_justify_recommendation_model_rollout

ground_truth_rationale: The model is technically superior in CTR, but the deployment decision must account for commercial obligations (merchant agreements) and catalog health (long-tail diversity), neither of which is captured in CTR or offline metrics. The mixed verdict recognizes genuine model quality while identifying a pre-rollout audit requirement that is specific to this business context.
notes: Mixed position case. Recommendation system deployment with contractual constraints. 2 must-find issues. Multiple acceptable resolutions.

task_prompt: A consumer bank's fraud operations team evaluated a neural network-based transaction fraud detection model over a 6-month retrospective period. During the lookback, the model was applied post-hoc to all 220 million transactions processed by the bank, and fraud analysts compared model scores against confirmed fraud labels from the bank's existing dispute resolution and investigation system. The retrospective analysis found the model would have flagged 91% of confirmed fraud cases (sensitivity 0.91) at a false positive rate of 0.8% (specificity 0.992). The operations team proposes deploying the model as a hard block—any transaction scoring above the deployment threshold is automatically declined without customer notification, without a decline reason code, and without any manual review pathway—citing the high sensitivity and low false positive rate as justification. The current fraud loss rate is $2.10 per $1,000 transacted. False declines in the existing manual review system generate customer complaints at a rate of 0.6 per 1,000 declined transactions. The bank serves a disproportionately high share of underbanked customers, defined as individuals who rely heavily on their bank account as their primary financial access point and have limited alternative payment methods such as credit cards, digital wallets, or overdraft coverage. At 220 million transactions per period, a 0.8% false positive rate translates to 1.76 million automated false declines.
